title: Beyond Bloom's: How Fink's Taxonomy Transforms CS Course Design
summary: Bloom's tells you how hard the thinking is. Fink's tells you whether it matters to the person doing the thinking. Explore the six dimensions of significant learning and why they matter in the age of AI.
content_plain:
Bloom's tells you how hard the thinking is. Fink's tells you whether it matters to the person doing the thinking. Explore the six dimensions of significant learning and why they matter in the age of AI. If you have been in higher education for a while, you have probably encountered Bloom's Taxonomy: Remember, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate, Create.
The cognitive hierarchy that tells you whether you are asking students to think deeply or just recall facts.
Bloom's is genuinely useful. But L. Dee Fink, in his 2013 book Creating Significant Learning Experiences, noticed something important. Bloom's only addresses one dimension of how humans learn. It tells you how cognitively demanding a task is. It says almost nothing about whether students will care about it, connect it to anything real, or know how to keep learning on their own after the course ends.
For computing and STEM faculty specifically, this gap has real consequences. We spend enormous energy designing cognitively rigorous assessments and then wonder why students who passed the exam still struggle to function in an internship.
Bloom's tells you how hard the thinking is. Fink's tells you whether it matters to the person doing the thinking.
The Six Dimensions of Fink's Taxonomy
Fink's taxonomy is not a hierarchy. It is an interactive web where every dimension strengthens the others.
 Dimension What It Means In a Computing Course 
 
 Foundational Knowledge (FK) Understanding and remembering key concepts Data structures, algorithm analysis, language syntax 
 Application (AP) Critical thinking, practical skills, managing projects Implementing algorithms, debugging, system design 
 Integration (IN) Connecting ideas across subjects and life contexts Seeing how OS concepts relate to security 
 Human Dimension (HD) Learning about oneself and others Code review etiquette, equity, accessibility awareness 
 Caring (CA) Developing new interests, values, or feelings Caring about software quality, open-source ethics 
 Learning How to Learn (LL) Metacognition and self-direction Reading documentation, developing a debugging mindset 
Why This Matters Right Now
AI is extraordinarily good at Foundational Knowledge. It can explain recursion, walk through a sorting algorithm, or produce syntactically correct code on demand. If your assessments primarily target FK, students do not need to engage with the material.
But AI cannot learn to care about software quality on your student's behalf. It cannot develop their debugging mindset. The upper dimensions of Fink's taxonomy—HD, CA, and LL—are exactly where human learning is irreplaceable.
The AI Audit
For each of your major assessments, ask yourself: which Fink dimensions does this assignment actually require the student to engage? If you only see FK and AP, you have found your highest-risk assignment for AI substitution.
Practical Examples: Fink in a CS Course
Consider a standard data structures assignment: "Implement a binary search tree with insert, delete, and search operations."
This targets Foundational Knowledge and Application. Now add one question: "Describe a real system you use daily that likely relies on a tree structure. How does your implementation compare to what you would expect in production?"
That one addition requires Integration, Human Dimension, Caring, and Learning How to Learn. AI can generate plausible-sounding text—but the student still has to have had the experience to write something genuine.
Getting Started: The Fink Audit
Take your next major assignment and map it against the six dimensions. Which dimensions does it genuinely require? Which are completely absent? Then ask yourself: what is the smallest possible change that would add one missing dimension?
References
Anderson, L.W. and Krathwohl, D.R. (Eds.). (2001). A taxonomy for learning, teaching, and assessing. Longman.
Fink, L.D. (2003). Creating significant learning experiences. Jossey-Bass.

title: Four Strategies for High-Impact Feedback Without Burning Out
summary: Research-backed approaches to providing specific, humane, and sustainable feedback using rubrics, screencasts, tiered strategies, and thoughtful AI tools.
content_plain:
Research-backed approaches to providing specific, humane, and sustainable feedback using rubrics, screencasts, tiered strategies, and thoughtful AI tools. By this point in the semester, the honeymoon is over. Students have hit their first complex coding project and many of them are hitting a wall. The feedback you give at this moment does real work.
Research tells us that feedback is most effective when it's specific, timely, and actionable (Shute, 2008). But in STEM courses, we also need it to be efficient and accessible.
Strategy 1: Make Rubrics Do the Heavy Lifting
Well-designed rubrics turn vague judgment into transparent expectations. When rubrics include descriptive performance levels and are shared before students begin the work, they improve learning outcomes and student satisfaction.
Three moves you can implement right now:
Align each rubric criterion with a specific learning objective and an authentic professional practice such as test coverage, branching strategy, or secure queries.
Share the rubric when you assign the work and ask students to self-assess against it before submission. This supports metacognition and improves performance.
In advanced courses, co-create parts of the rubric by asking students what distinguishes novice work from expert work.
Strategy 2: 120-Second Over-the-Shoulder Screencast Reviews
Some problems are just inefficient to explain in text. A subtle logic error or a flawed circuit diagram is often easier to show than describe.
Open the student's code or artifact. Start a quick screencast. Spend two focused minutes highlighting strengths and one to three priority issues.
"I see what you're doing here on line 42. You're reinitializing the counter inside the loop, which is why it never increments."
Frame your comments at three levels:
Task: what is happening and why it is incorrect.
Process: strategies the student can reuse in future work.
Self-regulation: reinforcement of productive approaches they are already using.
Strategy 3: Tiered Feedback So You Don't Burn Out
Not every student needs the same type or depth of feedback.
Top 20 percent: Brief text comments highlighting exemplary practices. Time per student: two to three minutes.
Middle 60 percent: Five- to seven-minute screencasts focused on two or three rubric criteria.
Bottom 20 percent: Screencast plus an invitation to meet, emphasizing growth and support.
This typically adds up to about three hours of feedback time—comparable to detailed written comments but with higher instructional payoff.
Strategy 4: Thoughtful Use of AI for Feedback
AI tools can reduce routine grading work and provide fast correctness feedback, but they are not substitutes for professional judgment.
Use AI to:
Run test suites and identify edge cases. Enforce style and basic security rules. Provide immediate, low-stakes feedback for iteration.
Reserve human review for:
Architecture and design choices. Maintainability and documentation. Security practices and professional conventions.
References
Hattie, J., & Timperley, H. (2007). The power of feedback. Review of Educational Research, 77(1), 81–112.
Shute, V. J. (2008). Focus on formative feedback. Review of Educational Research, 78(1), 153–189.

title: Making Your Course Matter: Three Strategies for Industry Relevance
summary: Students aren't unmotivated—they can't see how coursework connects to real work. Use certifications, job descriptions, and current tech news to make the connection explicit.
content_plain:
Students aren't unmotivated—they can't see how coursework connects to real work. Use certifications, job descriptions, and current tech news to make the connection explicit. Let me be direct. Week three is when you lose them.
The novelty has worn off. Students are juggling five courses, jobs, and various responsibilities. And at least one student is thinking, "When am I ever going to use Big-O notation in real life?"
The research is clear: when students cannot explain how coursework connects to professional practice, they are more likely to disengage or change majors (Margolis & Fisher, 2002). This is not a motivation problem. It is a visibility problem.
Strategy 1: Cite Industry Certifications in Your Assignments
Industry certifications represent consensus about what practitioners are expected to know. They are public, specific, and regularly updated.
The 15-minute transformation
Take an existing technical assignment and add two things: the certification exam domain it aligns with, and a realistic professional scenario.
Before: "Implement a binary search tree with insert, delete, and search operations."
After: "The AWS Certified Developer exam, Domain 2.3, requires understanding data structure selection for application optimization. You are a junior developer evaluating data structures for a real-time leaderboard that processes 50,000 updates per minute. Implement a binary search tree, then write a one-page technical memo recommending whether this structure fits the use case."
Strategy 2: Let Students Reverse-Engineer Job Descriptions
Pull 4–6 job postings from LinkedIn. Mix experience levels. Choose companies students recognize.
Part 1: Analysis (15 min) — Students work in groups, categorize requirements into technical skills, tools, soft skills.
Part 2: Pattern Recognition (10 min) — Students are often surprised to see communication and documentation listed in 70–80% of technical roles.
Part 3: Course Mapping (15 min) — Close the loop explicitly. "This week's database design project addresses the 'design scalable data schemas' requirement that appeared in every data engineering position we analyzed."
Strategy 3: Teach With This Week's Tech News
Students already follow tech news. When coursework connects to headlines, relevance becomes immediate.
A practical system:
Set up a news radar — Create Google Alerts for your language or field plus terms like vulnerability or breach.
Build flexibility into your syllabus — Designate responsive assignments that can pivot based on current events.
Move quickly — Current events have a two-week relevance window.
The Bottom Line: Students are not unmotivated. They cannot see how coursework connects to what they care about. You do not need to rebuild your course. You need to make visible the connections that experienced faculty already see.
Start small. Revise one assignment. Run one activity. Set up one alert.

title: The Flipped Tech Classroom: From Passive Lectures to Active Learning
summary: How to design effective pre-class content, maximize in-class application, and solve the 'they didn't watch the video' problem with evidence-based strategies.
content_plain:
How to design effective pre-class content, maximize in-class application, and solve the 'they didn't watch the video' problem with evidence-based strategies. Now that students are comfortable navigating the tools, Week 2 presents the perfect opportunity to establish active learning norms through the Flipped Classroom model.
The flipped model moves passive content delivery online, reserving the classroom for high-intensity problem-solving. A meta-analysis of 225 studies found that active learning environments improved exam scores by 6% and reduced failure rates by 1.5x (Freeman et al., 2014).
Phase 1: Designing Effective Pre-Class Content
The success of your Tuesday lab depends entirely on what your students did Monday night.
The Segmentation Principle: Keep videos between 6-9 minutes. Attention drops off a cliff after the 6-minute mark.
The Worked Example Principle: Students learn better by studying an expert's process than by being thrown into a problem without a map.
Leverage the Global Tech Community
Don't reinvent the wheel. If a world-class engineer has already explained Binary Search Trees with high-end animations, use their expertise. Your value is not in being a "broadcaster," but in being the editor-in-chief who selects the most accurate resources.
Phase 2: Maximizing In-Class Application
- Think-Pair-Share Code Reviews (20 min): Present a "dirty" Python function. 3 minutes to find the bugs, 7 minutes to refactor with a partner, 10 minutes to debate the "cleanest" solution.
- Mini-Project Sprints (40 min): Assign a "Ticket." "Our API is slow. We have 500k records. Fix it." Teams must diagnose, prototype, and document.
The Achilles Heel: "They Didn't Watch the Video"
The most common reason flipped classrooms fail is students arriving unprepared.
Use the "Testing Effect" — Package your videos with interactive questions. Students who answer questions inserted between short video segments perform significantly better.
The Low-Stakes Quiz: Make a 5-question quiz due 2 hours before class. This increases viewing rates from 60% to nearly 90%.
The "Entrance Ticket": Students must submit one specific technical question about the video before they can join the day's group activity.
The Flipped Tech Class Contract
In this course, we use a Flipped Learning Model. This mirrors the industry reality of "Continuous Learning": a Senior Developer is expected to read the documentation before attending the Sprint Planning.
- Your Responsibility: Engage with the short video modules and complete the "Entrance Quiz."
- My Responsibility: Ensure class time is never a repeat of the video.
- The Reality Check: If you come prepared, you will build a portfolio of work during class hours.

title: Week 1: Setting the Tech Tone for Your Course
summary: The first week isn't about content—it's about helping students master the course's 'operating system' so they can focus on learning your discipline.
content_plain:
The first week isn't about content—it's about helping students master the course's 'operating system' so they can focus on learning your discipline. The first week of any course sets the trajectory for the entire semester. In computing and technology courses, where students must navigate not only disciplinary content but also a complex technology stack, clarity from day one is a necessity.
Research demonstrates that students' initial interactions with course technology significantly predict their subsequent engagement and success (Emelyanova & Voronina, 2014).
Evidence-Based Strategies
Create a Visual Technology Roadmap
Students benefit from a clear, shared picture of where things live—where to access readings, submit work, participate in discussions, and get help. A simple one-page guide can reduce confusion across any course.
Active First-Day Engagement
Students learn systems by using them. Instead of explaining tools, design first-day activities that require students to post, upload, annotate, or access course materials.
Early Support Using LMS Data
Students who do not engage in Week 1 are at higher risk of struggling later (Macfadyen & Dawson, 2010). A quick check-in of activity can prompt timely, supportive outreach.
When students feel confident with course technology, they can invest their energy in the discipline itself.
The Week One Payoff
By investing intentional effort in technological clarity during week one, you create what educational psychologists call "self-efficacy"—students' belief in their ability to succeed (Bandura, 1997). When students feel confident navigating the technology, they can focus cognitive resources on learning your discipline rather than fighting with unfamiliar platforms.
References
Arnold, K. E., & Pistilli, M. D. (2012). Course signals at Purdue. Proceedings of the 2nd International Conference on Learning Analytics and Knowledge, 267-270.
Bandura, A. (1997). Self-efficacy: The exercise of control. W.H. Freeman.

title: Books for Joyful Teaching: A Curated Reading List for Faculty
summary: A treasure trove of resources arguing for teaching as an act of hope, care, and human flourishing—compiled from recommendations by experienced educators.
content_plain:
A treasure trove of resources arguing for teaching as an act of hope, care, and human flourishing—compiled from recommendations by experienced educators. Faculty across higher education have been grappling with unprecedented challenges: from navigating rapid technological changes and AI integration to addressing student mental health concerns. Yet amid these difficulties, educators are increasingly recognizing that joy, care, and hope aren't luxuries in teaching—they're essential to sustainable, transformative pedagogy.
Essential Reads for Joy-Centered Teaching
Joy-Centered Pedagogy in Higher Education
Lori Gonzalez, Editor (Open Access)
This collaborative collection stands out for its accessibility and practical wisdom. Editor Lori Gonzalez structured the volume with "reader reflection" questions at the end of each chapter and a compilation of teaching tips that serve as perfect discussion fodder for book clubs.
A Pedagogy of Kindness
by Catherine Denial
This book "prompted us to challenge our assumptions about 'rigor' and 'good teaching' and to rethink our classroom practices to foster meaningful learning, and student and faculty success."
Institutional Transformation and Care
Hope Circuits
by Lee Skallerup Bessette
Written for educators "trying to do hopeful, joyful, justice-oriented work inside institutions that often feel like they're fraying," this book tackles the gap between our aspirations for higher education and institutional realities.
The Caring University
by Kevin McClure
McClure provides a research-informed account of how institutions can center care—not as empty rhetoric, but as "a set of practices, structures, and leadership habits that make joy and belonging possible."
The Science of Emotional Engagement
The Spark of Learning
by Sarah Rose Cavanagh
Cavanagh's central argument: emotions are contagious, and "conveying passion, enthusiasm, and love for learning in the classroom is the best route to motivating student engagement."
What emerges from this collection is a vision of teaching that refuses to choose between rigor and care, between justice and joy. These authors collectively argue that in times of crisis, joy isn't a distraction from serious pedagogical work—it's essential to it.

title: A Curated Guide to AI Resources for CS Faculty
summary: The internet is flooded with AI resources. This is a practical, curated guide to help you find the most useful ones for your courses without the noise.
content_plain:
The internet is flooded with AI resources. This is a practical, curated guide to help you find the most useful ones for your courses without the noise. As faculty, you are in a unique position. You aren't just figuring out how to use Generative AI—you are teaching the students who will build, deploy, and critically evaluate these tools for years to come.
The challenge is twofold: How can you leverage AI to improve your own teaching? And how can you effectively integrate AI into your curriculum as a core competency?
Start with IU: Key Local Resources
Generative AI 101 Faculty Resources — An overview of the GenAI 101 Course available to all at IU, including a syllabus insert to promote the course to students.
Kelley School of Business "AI Playbook" — A "living guide" for faculty on the use of generative AI in teaching, grading, and research.
Three Actionable Resources to Start
1. Teach CS with AI — A hub specifically for integrating AI into CS courses with lesson plans, project ideas, and pedagogical strategies.
2. Harvard "Teaching with Gen-AI" Resources — High-level guidance on course design, with case studies and strategies for handling risks like hallucinations.
3. AI for Education: "Effective Prompting for Educators" — Frameworks like the "5 S Framework" for teaching students structured prompt engineering.
Deep Dive: Using AI in Your Classroom
- Derek Bok Center (Harvard): "Teaching in the Age of AI" — Concrete assignment ideas and sample activities.
- One Useful Thing: "15 Times to Use AI and 5 Not To" — Where AI is helpful and where it isn't.
- Syllabi Policies for AI Generative Tools — Sample policies to help develop your own.
Helping Students Get Better at Prompting
- AI for Education: Prompt Library — Searchable collection of ready-to-use prompts for educators.
- More Useful Things Prompt Repository — Tested prompt sets from researchers Ethan and Lilach Mollick.
- Anthropic Prompt Library — "What good prompting looks like" from an industry leader.
Teaching AI in CS Courses
- metaLAB at Harvard: AI Pedagogy Project — Assignments for critical AI thinking.
- Ideeas Lab — Resources tailored for engineering domains.
- AI for Education: Critical Analysis Activities — Activities for students to evaluate AI rather than simply use it.
Building Your Own AI Tools
- HBSP: "How to Create Custom AI Chatbots" — Walk-through for designing custom AI chatbots for classroom engagement.
- OpenAI: "A Practical Guide to Building Agents" — Technical guide for building and deploying AI agents.
 Conclusion: You don't need to redesign your entire curriculum overnight. Pick one thing to try this month.

title: Teaching for Integrity in the Age of AI: From Compliance to Culture
summary: Academic integrity is not a checklist. It is a living culture shaped by what we model, how we design, and the conversations we hold with our students—especially now that AI is everywhere.
content_plain:
Academic integrity is not a checklist. It is a living culture shaped by what we model, how we design, and the conversations we hold with our students—especially now that AI is everywhere. Inspired by Chapter 2 of The Opposite of Cheating: Teaching for Integrity in the Age of AI by Tricia Bertram Gallant and David Rettinger
Academic integrity is not a checklist or compliance form. It is a living culture shaped by what we model, how we design, and the conversations we hold with our students. Gallant and Rettinger remind us that integrity is cultivated through transparency, design, and dialogue, not surveillance or punishment. The real challenge now is how to teach integrity in an age where AI is everywhere.
2025 Snapshot: AI & Academic Integrity
Use and Attitudes
- Over 85% of undergraduates use GenAI tools
- 61% of students want clear, course-level AI policies
- 33% of students were concerned about being accused of plagiarism or cheating
- While 45% believe using AI for editing is "acceptable academic support"
Institutional Responses
- Less than 40% of higher education institutions have formal AI acceptable use policies
- AI-related misconduct tripled in the UK
- 76% of universities are redesigning assessments for AI contexts
Faculty Trends
- A significant gap exists between student and faculty adoption: only 61% of faculty report using AI in teaching
- 82% of instructors use GenAI for feedback or rubric design
- Detection tools now use watermarking and metadata tracing, but false positives remain a major concern
Model Integrity
Students notice how we work. They learn from the way we check our sources, document decisions, and acknowledge mistakes. Modeling integrity starts with transparency.
When instructors frame AI as a learning partner, not a shortcut, students develop stronger judgment and accountability.
Make Integrity Explicit
Integrity should show up as often in our discussions as it does in our policies. When we talk about it before projects, during collaborations, and after challenges, students begin to see ethics as part of the learning process.
Use Clear, Simple Language
Students need to understand AI policies to be able to follow them. That means avoiding jargon and overly technical language.
Instead of: "AI assistance must align with established academic integrity principles."
Say: "You may use AI for brainstorming ideas, but not for writing entire sections of code or essays."
Frame Integrity Positively
Instead of framing integrity around rules, frame it around growth. Students respond better when they see ethical choices as part of their professional development.
Clarify Expectations
Ambiguity creates rationalization. In the age of AI, clarity is an act of fairness. Faculty can address this by defining boundaries early and discussing examples.
Use the Syllabus as a Moral Document
The syllabus sets the tone for integrity. Transparent grading policies, clear AI statements, and flexible revision options communicate fairness and care.
Final Thought
Teaching for integrity in the age of AI is about creating conditions where honesty becomes the natural choice. When we model transparency, design for trust, and engage in open dialogue, we teach more than content—we teach character.
"Integrity is not the opposite of cheating. It's the presence of purpose."

title: Possible Ways to Improve Attendance and Clarification on Last Week's Post
summary: Practical, evidence-based strategies to re-engage students who aren't coming to class—from combating anonymity to creating incentive, relevance, and engagement.
content_plain:
Practical, evidence-based strategies to re-engage students who aren't coming to class—from combating anonymity to creating incentive, relevance, and engagement. One of the most frequent concerns I hear is, "My students just aren't coming to class." With so much content available online, recorded lectures at their fingertips, and the sense of distance that can come with large classes, this challenge is becoming more common and more complex.
The Anonymity Epidemic: When Students Feel Like Just Another Face
Many students, particularly in large enrollment courses, feel anonymous. They don't believe their individual presence makes a difference, leading to a disengagement from the classroom community.
Strategies to Combat Anonymity:
- Be Present Before Class: Arriving early to chat informally with students is a simple yet powerful way to build rapport.
- Active Engagement is Key: Design activities that actively involve students with the material. Pose intriguing questions, facilitate brief peer discussions, or utilize classroom response systems.
- Learn Their Names (or Try): Even the attempt to learn student names is deeply appreciated. A study found that students' perception of their instructor knowing their name was highly correlated with a sense of belonging.
- Cultivate Peer Connections: Encourage students to get to know each other. On the first day, have them introduce themselves to those around them.
- Personalized Feedback (Even in Large Classes): Think in layers—macro, meso, and micro feedback.
The "Why Bother?" Dilemma: Lack of Incentive, Relevance, and Engagement
Strategies to Create Incentive and Relevance:
- Incentivize Attendance: Make attendance a component of the grade, or administer short, low-stakes quizzes at the beginning of class.
- Design Slides to Drive Presence: Explicitly state that your posted slides are incomplete. Design them as skeletal frameworks.
- Connect to Their World: Embed examples and topics that resonate with students' fields of study.
- Pique Interest from the Start: Begin lectures with a challenging question, an intriguing anecdote, or a real-world problem.
- Convey Your Enthusiasm: Your passion for the subject is contagious!
Overcoming Information Overload
- Chunk Your Lectures & Re-engage: Plan your lectures in shorter chunks, incorporating varied activities every 15-20 minutes.
- Complement, Don't Reiterate, the Textbook: Use class time to expand on readings, provide alternative perspectives.
- Provide Unique Experiences: Bring in guest speakers, conduct live demonstrations.
The Power of Visuals and Storytelling
- Integrate Visualizations: Use diagrams, flowcharts, and animations. Show, don't just tell.
- Tell Stories of Impact: Frame technical concepts within the context of real-world problems.
- Live Coding Demonstrations: Allow students to see the process, observe debugging strategies.
By adopting these evidence-based strategies, faculty can transform their lectures from passive information dissemination into vibrant, engaging learning experiences that students genuinely want to attend.

title: Navigating Attendance Challenges in the Modern Classroom: Practical Tips for Faculty
summary: Policy-aligned tips and strategies for navigating common attendance scenarios—from students who email before exams claiming illness to those who miss multiple assessments.
content_plain:
Policy-aligned tips and strategies for navigating common attendance scenarios—from students who email before exams claiming illness to those who miss multiple assessments. Managing attendance has always been a balancing act; but in today's teaching landscape, it's more like walking a tightrope. Between health-related absences, caregiving responsibilities, flexible attendance accommodations, and student disengagement, it can feel increasingly difficult to find the right mix of empathy, fairness, and structure.
1. Move from Policing to Partnership
Faculty cannot (and should not) request or require doctor's notes or other forms of medical documentation. Instead, redirect students to official university support channels:
- Student Care and Resource Center (SCRC): For extended absences due to hospitalization, family emergencies, or crises.
- Accessible Educational Services (AES): For ongoing or temporary medical conditions that interfere with coursework.
Tip: Keep a short paragraph in your syllabus:
"If health or personal challenges interfere with your coursework, please contact the Student Care and Resource Center or Accessible Educational Services. They can help coordinate official support so we can make appropriate adjustments."
2. Redefine Attendance as Engagement
Consider measuring engagement instead of simple presence:
- Submitting reflection prompts or "exit tickets" after class
- Participating in online discussion threads or collaborative documents
- Contributing to group projects or peer reviews
- Completing brief "pre-class" or "after-class" questions in Canvas
3. Responding to Common Scenarios
When a student emails right before the exam claiming illness:
"Thank you for letting me know. I hope you're feeling better soon. Please take the time you need to recover. Once you're able, check the course site for next steps or reach out so we can discuss options for making up missed work, consistent with the course policy."
When a student simply forgets the exam:
"I understand mistakes happen, but make-up exams are reserved for documented emergencies. Unfortunately, forgetting the exam time doesn't qualify for a make-up opportunity."
When a student misses multiple assessments but is still partially engaged:
1. Flag the student via the Student Engagement Roster (SER)
2. Send a check-in message
3. Offer a structured path forward
4. Balancing Fairness and Flexibility
Fairness does not mean treating every student identically—it means providing equitable opportunities to succeed. Transparency is key.
5. Practical Prevention Strategies
- Set clear expectations early
- Communicate the value of class time
- Make learning visible with exit tickets
- Reach out early when you notice patterns
- Model reliability
Sample Missed Exam Policy
Each student has one "illness/emergency token" per term for a major assessment. The make-up must be completed within 5 business days of the original exam. Only one major assessment may be made up via the token. For low-stakes work, drop the lowest 2 quizzes.
Final Thought: Attendance challenges often reveal deeper student struggles. The goal is not to eliminate every absence, but to build a culture of engagement and accountability.

title: Building a Framework for Academia-Industry Partnerships and AI Teaching Podcasts
summary: Exploring the CRA report on academia-industry relationships, plus curated AI teaching and learning podcasts for faculty.
content_plain:
Exploring the CRA report on academia-industry relationships, plus curated AI teaching and learning podcasts for faculty. The CRA-Education / CRA-Industry working group recently released a report titled Breadth of Practices in Academia-Industry Relationships which explores engagement models from research partnerships and personnel exchanges to master agreements and regional innovation ecosystems.
Key Findings and Observations
The report organizes its findings into three categories:
- Observations: A major theme was the critical need to embed ethical training into AI and computing curricula through both standalone courses and integrated assignments.
- Barriers: Key barriers include cultural differences between academic and industry partners, and the prohibitive cost of cloud and GPU hardware.
- Common Solutions: Effective solutions include fluid movement of personnel through internships, co-ops, sabbaticals, and dual appointments.
Strategies for Research Collaboration
- Individuals: Faculty can initiate relationships through seed grants, sabbaticals in industry, and advisory boards.
- Departments: Leaders can match faculty expertise with industry needs and build a strong departmental brand.
- University Leadership: Senior leaders can create unified strategies and implement master agreements.
- Regional Ecosystems: Universities partnering with local industries to build innovation ecosystems.
Recommendations
1. Enhance research impact by combining academia's long-term vision with real-world problems from industry.
2. Leverage the convening power of universities to build partnerships.
3. Accelerate workforce development by aligning programs with regional ecosystems.
4. Deliver industry-relevant curricula grounded in core computing principles.
5. Establish new incentives and metrics for faculty contributions to industry partnerships.

title: The Guide on the Side: Coaching STEM Students in Problem-Solving
summary: Equip your teaching assistants with high-leverage strategies to coach students through problem-solving rather than simply providing answers.
content_plain:
Equip your teaching assistants with high-leverage strategies to coach students through problem-solving rather than simply providing answers. It's a familiar scene in any STEM lab or office hour: a student, staring at a screen, is utterly stuck. For new teaching assistants, the temptation is strong to simply provide the answer. But while this solves the immediate problem, it bypasses a crucial learning opportunity.
Why This Shift in Pedagogy Matters
When an instructor or AI simply hands over the solution, the student leaves with a single answer but no transferable skill. By contrast, an instructor who guides the process models resilience, inquiry, and expert reasoning. The student leaves not only with a solution but with strategies they can apply to the next problem.
Putting Theory into Practice
Faculty can use these activities in training sessions:
- "Sticking Point" Brainstorm: In a think-pair-share format, AIs identify the most common places their students struggle.
- Scenario Analysis: AIs compare contrasting dialogues—one where the AI gives the answer directly, and another using Socratic questioning.
- Questioning Roleplay: AIs practice responding with guiding questions when students say "I'm totally lost."
A Simple Framework for Modeling Expertise
1. Understand: Restate the problem in your own words. What are the inputs, outputs, and constraints?
2. Plan: Outline possible approaches. What tools or algorithms might be useful?
3. Do: Execute step by step, narrating the reasoning behind each action.
4. Reflect: Test the solution. Does it work for edge cases? Could it be more efficient?
The Power of a Good Question
- To start: "What have you tried so far?" or "Can you walk me through your current approach?"
- To prompt a next step: "What does that error message suggest?"
- To encourage deeper thinking: "Why did you choose that particular method?"
- To promote reflection: "How could you check your answer?"
Navigating Common Challenges
- When a student is silent: Allow for sufficient wait time. If it persists, break the problem down.
- When a student is frustrated: Acknowledge their feelings and normalize the struggle.
- When a student just wants confirmation: Redirect with a metacognitive prompt.
The result is a more engaged and resilient cohort of students who leave your courses not only with solutions, but with the confidence and strategies to tackle the next challenge independently.

title: Best Practices for Working with Assistant Instructors
summary: Recommendations for building a strong faculty–assistant instructor partnership, from setting expectations before the semester to supporting professional development.
content_plain:
Recommendations for building a strong faculty–assistant instructor partnership, from setting expectations before the semester to supporting professional development. Assistant instructors (AIs) can play an essential role in supporting your course. They support student learning, enhance faculty efficiency, and gain valuable professional development experience. When managed thoughtfully, the faculty-assistant instructor partnership creates a stronger learning environment.
Core Principles of a Strong Partnership
- Clear Expectations and Roles: Both faculty and assistant instructors need a shared understanding of their responsibilities.
- Faculty as the Ultimate Authority: Faculty ultimately carry responsibility for course administration duties, including grading.
- Professional Development Opportunity: Connect assigned tasks to professional growth, teaching skills, and career preparation.
- Consistent Communication: Regular check-ins and transparency help prevent misunderstandings.
Setting Up for Success
Before the Semester Begins
Meet with your assistant instructor before classes start to cover:
- Course goals and learning outcomes
- Roles, tasks, and boundaries
- Meeting schedules and communication channels
- Workload expectations (respecting weekly hour limits)
- Familiarity with technology tools
- Academic integrity policies
During the Semester
- Regular Meetings: Weekly or biweekly to prepare for lessons and review grading approaches.
- Grading Consistency: Provide rubrics and sample feedback. Calibration activities are especially effective.
- Office Hours: Encourage consistent and accessible office hours at different times of day.
- Mid-Semester Check-In: Gather feedback, review workloads, and adjust if necessary.
End of the Semester
Wrap up with a reflective meeting. Discuss what worked well, identify challenges, and preserve useful materials for future iterations.
Supporting AI Development
Faculty aren't just supervisors—they're mentors:
- Coach on teaching strategies and classroom management
- Encourage professional development goals and teaching portfolio building
- Provide opportunities for peer observation and self-reflection
- Direct to school and university-wide teaching resources

title: Teaching Tip: What Are You Really Trying to Assess?
summary: Too often, assessments measure peripheral skills like memorization rather than intended learning outcomes. Evidence-based practices to align assessments with your goals.
content_plain:
Too often, assessments measure peripheral skills like memorization rather than intended learning outcomes. Evidence-based practices to align assessments with your goals. As you design quizzes, projects, and exams, it's worth pausing to ask: What am I really trying to assess? Too often, assessments measure peripheral skills like memorization, rather than the intended learning outcomes.
Evidence-based practices to align assessments with your goals:
1. Backwards Design (Wiggins & McTighe, 2005)
 - Start with the outcome: Do you want students to demonstrate abstraction, debugging, empathy for users, or system-level thinking?
 - Then design an assessment that directly elicits that performance.
2. Constructive Alignment (Biggs, 1996)
 - Ensure that learning activities, assessments, and outcomes are in sync.
3. Authentic Assessment (Herrington & Herrington, 2007)
 - Use real-world tasks (e.g., designing a database for a case study client, creating a usability test plan).
4. Reduce Construct-Irrelevant Barriers
 - If the skill being assessed is debugging, provide starter code so students aren't penalized for setup.
When Expectations Are Unclear
Students struggle not because the concepts are beyond their ability, but because the expectations of the assessment are unclear.
- Cognitive Load: Ambiguous assessments create unnecessary cognitive load (Sweller, 2011).
- Equity Impact: Lack of clarity disproportionately disadvantages first-generation students.
- Misalignment: Vague assessments often misalign with course outcomes.
What Faculty Can Do:
1. State the Core Construct: Ask: Am I assessing correctness, creativity, reasoning, or communication?
2. Communicate Priorities: Indicate relative weight (e.g., correctness 50%, efficiency 30%, documentation 20%).
3. Provide a Sample Response: A brief annotated example helps students see what you value.
4. Check for Hidden Criteria: If you penalize for style or teamwork, ensure that's written down.
Takeaway: Before finalizing any assignment, ask: Am I measuring the skill that truly matters, or something adjacent?

title: Name the Thinking (Cognitive Skill), Not Just the Task
summary: A quick teaching tip: take 1–2 minutes to make the thinking process explicit when introducing activities. Name the cognitive skill, not just the task.
content_plain:
A quick teaching tip: take 1–2 minutes to make the thinking process explicit when introducing activities. Name the cognitive skill, not just the task. When introducing a problem set, coding lab, or design activity, take 1–2 minutes to make the thinking process explicit. For example:
- Instead of just saying: "Debug this code"
Add: "This task is about identifying assumptions in how the code should work versus how it runs. Pay attention to the strategies you use: reading error messages, testing small chunks, or tracing variables."
- Instead of just saying: "Sketch a wireframe"
Add: "This is about perspective-taking—imagining the interface from a novice user's point of view."
By naming the cognitive skill (debugging, pattern recognition, abstraction, empathy, systems thinking), students begin to see how their work maps onto the broader competencies of your field.
Why it matters:
- Supports metacognition (students reflect on how they learn, not just what they learn).
- Helps novice learners connect class tasks to professional practices.
- Reinforces disciplinary literacies and makes hidden expectations visible.

title: Pedagogical Tips for the Start of the Semester
summary: Evidence-based strategies for the first weeks—from designing inclusive syllabi to establishing psychological safety and using early analytics for intervention.
content_plain:
Evidence-based strategies for the first weeks—from designing inclusive syllabi to establishing psychological safety and using early analytics for intervention. The first weeks of the semester are a unique window to shape not only what students will learn, but how they will learn. In STEM courses, where concepts can be abstract, skill levels vary wildly, and technologies evolve quickly, intentional, evidence-based practices can help you set students up for long-term success.
Design an Inclusive, Transparent Syllabus
Evidence base: Transparent teaching research (Winkelmes et al., 2016) shows that when students understand the purpose, tasks, and criteria for success, they perform better.
- Purpose statements: For every major assignment, include a short note on why it matters.
- Clear expectations: Break down grading policies into plain language.
- Accessibility & flexibility: Link to tutoring labs, office hours, and note-taking tools.
- Norm-setting: Add a "Community Norms" section covering respectful code reviews and group work.
Establish Psychological Safety Early
Evidence base: Google's Project Aristotle (2015) and Edmondson's (1999) work show that psychological safety is essential for high performance.
- Low stakes start: Run short, open-ended coding challenges in week one.
- Anonymous polls about programming experience to acknowledge diversity.
- Instructor vulnerability: Share a personal example of a bug or failed project.
- Model Constructive Feedback: Focus on growth and learning in early assignments.
Use Early Analytics for Intervention
- Student Engagement Roster (SER): Report non-attending students in the first week.
- Canvas Analytics: Identify struggling students through submissions and grades.
- Personal outreach: Email students who are slipping to connect them with tutoring.
- Positive nudges: Celebrate early wins.
Key Implementation Strategies
- Start Small and Build: Choose 2-3 strategies that align with your teaching style.
- Leverage Your Teaching Team: TAs are essential partners in large courses.
- Iterate Based on Data: Use student feedback and analytics to refine your approach.
- Maintain Connection at Scale: Use technology strategically for personal connection.
Remember: even small changes, consistently applied, can create significant improvements in student learning and engagement.

title: Digital Accessibility and Title II: What Tech Faculty Need to Know
summary: Federal accessibility requirements are changing. Here's what computing faculty need to know about Title II of the ADA and HEA, and how to lead inclusive change.
content_plain:
Federal accessibility requirements are changing. Here's what computing faculty need to know about Title II of the ADA and HEA, and how to lead inclusive change. As technological advancements reshape education, faculty in computing, engineering, data science, and information technology sit at the intersection of innovation and inclusion. Ensuring the digital environments we create are accessible for all learners is more than compliance—it's about shaping a future where every student can thrive.
Why Accessibility?
The data makes this clear:
- Around 28% of the US public reports having one or more disabilities
- 21% of undergraduates reported having a disability
- 30% of college students are diagnosed with anxiety and/or depression
- 12% have ADHD
- 4% have specific learning disabilities
There are four main reasons why you may not know who your students with disabilities are:
1. Most disabilities are invisible.
2. Students don't disclose due to fear of stigma.
3. Students may have a disability but don't have documentation.
4. Students with new conditions may not consider themselves as having a disability.
Understanding Title II: ADA + HEA
Title II of the ADA (1990, updated 2024): New rules require digital content and services to be accessible, including:
- Course content in your LMS
- Department websites and internal platforms
- Educational technologies used in class
- Videos, documents, and simulations
Key Deadline: April 2026 for institutions serving more than 50,000.
Content must be equally integrated, equally effective, and substantially equivalent in ease of use.
What Tech Faculty Can Do
1. Design Digitally Accessible Content from the Start
- Use alternative text (alt-text) for all images, charts, and graphs
- Caption all video and transcribe all audio content
- Structure documents with proper heading structures (H1, H2, H3)
- Use accessibility checkers in Word, Adobe Acrobat, or Google Docs
2. Evaluate the Accessibility of Tools and Platforms
- Check for WCAG 2.1 AA compliance before adopting new software
- Test for keyboard navigation and screen reader compatibility
- Leverage built-in LMS accessibility tools
3. Implement Inclusive Pedagogical Practices
- Use Universal Design for Learning (UDL) to offer multiple means of engagement
- Allow students to demonstrate learning in diverse ways
4. Track Outcomes and Improve with Data
- Align assignments to real-world certifications
- Use learning analytics to identify engagement gaps
Moving Forward
- Continuous Improvement: Regularly audit your courses with accessibility in mind.
- Collaborate: Partner with your institution's accessibility services.
- Educate Yourself and Others: Complete training on accessibility and UDL.
By aligning your teaching with Title II, you're building a future where every student can succeed in STEM and computing fields.

title: Supporting Non-Majors in Introductory Computer Courses
summary: Research on how students' majors influence their programming learning trajectories, and practical strategies for supporting non-CS students in intro courses.
content_plain:
Research on how students' majors influence their programming learning trajectories, and practical strategies for supporting non-CS students in intro courses. An increasing number of non-computer science students are now engaging in programming learning. However, they often struggle in early programming courses. Research analyzing data from students' learning processes helps us understand how a student's major influences their learning journey.
Key Findings
The study found that students' backgrounds and areas of study can affect how they approach and progress in learning programming. They suggest:
1. Making Programming Relevant: When teaching programming to non-CS students, connect the lessons to things important to them. For example, showing how programming can be used in art, music, or business.
2. Paying Extra Attention to Struggling Students: Teachers should keep a close eye on students who are not doing well. Connect them with teaching assistants and tutors early in the semester.
3. Using Tests to Track Progress: For non-CS students who are doing well on quizzes, the tests might not show their full abilities. Instructors need to consider other ways to evaluate their skills, not just based on smaller tests.
Implications for Teaching and Learning
- Tailored Instruction: Offer different learning paths based on a student's major. Students from a data science major might get advanced challenges while humanities students get more basic programming tasks.
- Early Support: Provide additional resources in the first few weeks for students from non-technical majors.
- Encouraging Diverse Majors: Organize workshops to show students from creative fields how programming can help them bring their ideas to life.
By understanding the relationship between a student's major and their programming learning trajectory, educators can create more effective and supportive learning environments.

title: Teaching Tip: What Are You Really Trying to Assess?
summary: Pause before designing your next exam to ask whether your assessments measure the intended learning outcomes—or peripheral skills like memorization and typing speed.
content_plain:
Pause before designing your next exam to ask whether your assessments measure the intended learning outcomes—or peripheral skills like memorization and typing speed. As you design quizzes, projects, and exams, it's worth pausing to ask: What am I really trying to assess? Too often, assessments measure peripheral skills like memorization, rather than the intended learning outcomes.
For example, a timed coding exam may end up evaluating typing speed and syntax recall more than algorithmic thinking or problem-solving strategy. Similarly, a multiple-choice exam on HCI principles may privilege memorization over the ability to apply design heuristics to new contexts.
Evidence-Based Practices to Align Assessments
1. Backwards Design (Wiggins & McTighe, 2005)
Start with the outcome: Do you want students to demonstrate abstraction, debugging, empathy for users, or system-level thinking? Then design an assessment that directly elicits that performance.
2. Constructive Alignment (Biggs, 1996)
Ensure that learning activities, assessments, and outcomes are in sync. If collaboration is a stated goal, include a group design critique, not just individual tests.
3. Minimize Construct-Irrelevant Variance
If you're testing problem-solving but time pressure dominates, the assessment may reflect test-taking speed more than understanding. Consider untimed or extended-time options.
4. Use Multiple Modes
Combine code submissions, reflective writing, oral explanations, and peer review to capture a fuller picture of student understanding.
5. Authentic Assessments
Design tasks that mirror real-world practice: debugging a codebase, reviewing a pull request, writing documentation, or presenting a design rationale.
Quick Self-Check
For your next assessment, ask:
- What learning outcome does this measure?
- Could a student succeed without demonstrating that outcome?
- What barriers might prevent a capable student from showing what they know?
References
Biggs, J. (1996). Enhancing teaching through constructive alignment. Higher Education, 32(3), 347–364.
Wiggins, G., & McTighe, J. (2005). Understanding by Design (2nd ed.). ASCD.

title: A Map Makes You Smarter, GPS Does Not: AI, Work, and What Comes Next
summary: Key insights from Jose Antonio Bowen's workshop on AI assignments and assessments—from cognitive offloading to raising the bar in the age of AI.
content_plain:
Key insights from Jose Antonio Bowen's workshop on AI assignments and assessments—from cognitive offloading to raising the bar in the age of AI. Jose Antonio Bowen, author of "Teaching Naked" and "Teaching with AI," provided a workshop on AI Assignment and Assessments where he mentioned:
"A map makes you smarter. GPS does not."
It was about how we're all starting to think less, remember less, and—if we're not careful—become less, all thanks to the technology we depend on.
The Decline of Entry-Level Everything
Shell laid off nearly 38% of a particular workforce group. Tasks that used to belong to interns—writing reports, creating slide decks, analyzing data—are now handled by AI.
The people who still have jobs are the ones who can think better than AI. Who can improve, refine, and oversee what AI produces.
Cognitive Offloading
We used to memorize phone numbers, navigate with paper maps, mentally calculate tips. Now we ask Siri. The scary part isn't forgetting how to do these things—it's what happens when we offload creativity, problem-solving, and thinking itself.
Creativity, Reimagined
Bowen showed how AI can spark creativity, not stifle it. Students could upload a 700-page textbook and have AI turn it into a nine-minute podcast. He talked about using AI to create personalized assignments: instead of a generic math problem, give a politics student a question about voter turnout rates.
Prompt Like a Pro
Don't just ask AI to "fix your proposal." Ask it to "transform your proposal into something your provost will love." Use emotion. Use intent. Give context.
The 70% Problem
AI can do a lot of things—but only up to a C-level standard. That's fine for a rough draft. It's dangerous for a final product.
The solution? Raise the bar.
What used to be acceptable for a B or C should now earn an F—unless the student can make the AI's work better, smarter, more human.
From Tools to Teaching Assistants
The future of education is not about banning AI—it's about designing with it. AI isn't replacing teachers—it's becoming part of the classroom, like textbooks once were.
"AI raises the floor. You must raise the ceiling."
Students need to surpass AI capabilities to be competitive in the job market, especially in fields like coding and writing.

title: Posit Connect: Building Interactive R Applications for Teaching and Research
summary: An overview of Shiny and Posit Connect at IU—how faculty can build interactive web applications using R for teaching statistics, data science, and research.
content_plain:
An overview of Shiny and Posit Connect at IU—how faculty can build interactive web applications using R for teaching statistics, data science, and research. Overview of Shiny & Posit Connect
- Shiny is a package in R (from Posit, formerly RStudio) that allows users to build interactive web applications using R.
- It's widely used for creating data visualizations and tools without needing web development skills.
- IU uses Posit Connect (formerly RStudio Connect) to host and share Shiny applications.
Why Use Shiny?
- Easy for R users to create apps for statistical analysis, data science, and teaching.
- Offers interactive elements like sliders and panels, which update visualizations in real time.
- Outputs render in browsers like standard web apps.
Posit Connect at IU
- IU has a central Posit Connect server that currently hosts apps for research and teaching.
- Teaching apps include interactive tools for statistics classes (e.g., manipulating effect size and observing graph changes).
- Research apps are used to share data tools and visualizations with broader communities.
Hosting Details
- Free Posit-hosted options are available for students and small-scale apps (under 5 apps or ~25 hrs/month).
- Currently supports around 70 users across various departments.
- Applications are limited to 100MB each.
Data & Security Guidelines
- Not approved for sensitive or restricted data (e.g., personal health info or identifiable student records).
- For secure data use cases, use IU's Tableau server or SecureMyResearch.
Examples of App Use
- Tools analyzing effect size vs. statistical power
- Genetic research tools and visualizations
- Social analytics dashboards comparing regional campuses
- Collaborations with external partners for data transparency projects
Contact: posit-connect@iu.edu

title: Pedagogical Tips for the Start of the Semester
summary: Evidence-based strategies for the first weeks: transparent syllabi, active learning from day one, early assessment, and building a classroom community.
content_plain:
Evidence-based strategies for the first weeks: transparent syllabi, active learning from day one, early assessment, and building a classroom community. The first weeks of the semester are a unique window to shape not only what students will learn, but how they will learn. In STEM courses, where concepts can be abstract, skill levels vary wildly, and technologies evolve quickly, intentional, evidence-based practices can help you set students up for long-term success.
Design an Inclusive, Transparent Syllabus
Transparent teaching research (Winkelmes et al., 2016) shows that when students understand the purpose, tasks, and criteria for success, they perform better.
- Purpose statements: For every major assignment, include a short note on why it matters and how it connects to industry or future coursework.
- Clear expectations: Break down grading policies, late work policies, and collaboration guidelines into plain language.
- Accessibility & flexibility: Link to tutoring labs, office hours, online learning resources, and note-taking tools.
Use Active Learning from Day One
Even simple techniques—think-pair-share, live polling, minute papers—signal that your class values participation over passive note-taking.
Conduct an Early, Low-Stakes Assessment
A brief diagnostic quiz or self-assessment helps you understand where students are starting from and helps students calibrate their own understanding.
Build a Classroom Community
- Learn names (or try)
- Use icebreakers that connect to course content
- Establish group norms for collaboration
- Create opportunities for peer-to-peer interaction early
Set Up Your LMS Intentionally
- Organize modules clearly with consistent naming
- Front-load the most important information
- Test all links and tools before students access them
The investment you make in the first two weeks pays dividends for the rest of the semester.

title: Bridging the Gap: What Tech Practitioners Really Want from CS Education
summary: Over 1,000 computing practitioners shared their honest thoughts on how well today's CS graduates are being prepared for the real world. Here's what they said.
content_plain:
Over 1,000 computing practitioners shared their honest thoughts on how well today's CS graduates are being prepared for the real world. Here's what they said. In the spring of 2024, the Computing Research Association (CRA) asked: What do industry professionals think about the way we teach computer science today?
More than 1,000 experienced computing practitioners—most with over two decades of experience—shared their honest thoughts.
The Call for More—and Better—Coursework
Students need more coursework in core CS subjects. Respondents recommended about four additional CS courses beyond what's typical today. Algorithms, computer architecture, and theoretical foundations topped the list.
They also suggested expanding foundational courses—especially in math, writing, and systems thinking.
It's Not Just About Programming
Practitioners agreed: learning to code isn't the end goal—learning to think like a problem-solver is.
They valued depth over breadth. Knowing one language well was seen as more important than dabbling in many. But they also stressed being adaptable.
The Soft Skills Shortfall
One of the most striking critiques was the lack of soft skills being taught. Oral communication courses were highlighted as a critical need. Liberal arts programs were doing a better job than engineering-focused ones in nurturing communication and collaboration.
Most important communication skills:
- Speaking confidently in small technical groups
- Writing solid technical documentation
- Explaining ideas clearly to both technical and non-technical audiences
Math Is Still a Must
65% of respondents said they enjoyed or pursued more math than required. The most important subjects: Statistics, linear algebra, discrete math, calculus, and logic.
Foundations First
- Algorithms: Emphasis on conceptual thinking, not just implementation.
- Computer Architecture: Digital logic and memory hierarchy were essential.
- Databases: Balance between theory (relational algebra, normalization) and practice (SQL, indexing).
Toward a Better Future
The message is clear: today's students need a curriculum that not only teaches them how to code, but prepares them to lead, adapt, and thrive in a complex, evolving world.

title: Title II Digital Accessibility and What It Means for Faculty
summary: An overview of Title II ADA and Title II HEA requirements for digital accessibility, with practical steps faculty can take to create inclusive learning environments.
content_plain:
An overview of Title II ADA and Title II HEA requirements for digital accessibility, with practical steps faculty can take to create inclusive learning environments. As technological advancements reshape education, faculty in computing, engineering, data science, and information technology sit at the intersection of innovation and inclusion. Ensuring the digital environments we create are accessible for all learners is more than compliance—it's about shaping a future where every student can thrive.
Why Accessibility?
The data makes this clear:
- According to 2022 CDC data, around 28% of the US public reports having one or more disabilities
- In a 2019-2020 survey, 21% of undergraduates and 11% of graduate students reported having a disability
- These percentages increase each year
Two Federal Statutes
Title II of the ADA requires that public entities, including public universities, ensure that their programs, services, and activities are accessible to individuals with disabilities—including all digital content.
Title II of the Higher Education Act (HEA) focuses on teacher preparation and professional development, including training educators to use accessible instructional materials and technology.
Practical Steps for Faculty
1. Use heading structures in documents and LMS pages
2. Add alt text to all images
3. Ensure color is not the only means of conveying information
4. Caption all videos and provide transcripts
5. Test your materials with accessibility checkers
6. Design assessments that don't create unnecessary barriers
7. Use accessible coding environments and tools
The Bigger Picture
When faculty in computing and STEM lead on accessibility, they model the inclusive design principles that students will carry into their careers. Accessibility is not an add-on—it's a design principle.

title: Mid-Semester Feedback and Google Illuminate: AI Tools for Faculty
summary: Using Google Illuminate to create podcast-style summaries of teaching resources, plus GenAI training courses for faculty and students.
content_plain:
Using Google Illuminate to create podcast-style summaries of teaching resources, plus GenAI training courses for faculty and students. It's a great time to think about receiving midsemester feedback on your course. This time I used a new resource that might be helpful:
Google Illuminate
Google Illuminate is an AI tool that creates podcast-style audio summaries of research papers. It's designed to make complex academic information more accessible and engaging.
How it works:
1. Input a research paper, PDF, or URL into Illuminate, or search for a topic.
2. Illuminate generates an audio summary that explains the key points.
3. You can ask questions about the document, and Illuminate will provide a text output.
4. You can convert the text output into a podcast answer.
I do NOT recommend inputting copyrighted information into this tool. However, it is optimized for computer science topics, and supports research papers hosted on arXiv.org
You might use this tool in your class to generate overviews of dense topics that students can listen to and/or read.
GenAI Essentials: Student-Facing Course
This course covers:
- Prompt Engineering – Crafting precise prompts to generate accurate outputs
- Evaluating AI-Generated Content – Assessing reliability, credibility, and biases
- Ethics and Limitations of GenAI – Understanding responsible AI use
- Information Literacy in a GenAI Age – Verification strategies for AI-generated sources
- Studying and Learning with GenAI – Using AI tools for note-taking and personalized learning
Faculty-Facing GenAI Course
The faculty version covers the same topics, but also includes assignment templates and rubrics that you can incorporate into your own course.

title: Supporting Non-Majors in Introductory Computer Courses
summary: Research on how students' academic backgrounds affect programming learning trajectories, with strategies for supporting non-CS majors in introductory courses.
content_plain:
Research on how students' academic backgrounds affect programming learning trajectories, with strategies for supporting non-CS majors in introductory courses. A study titled "Exploring Relations between Programming Learning Trajectories and Students' Majors" investigates how students from various academic disciplines learn programming in a compulsory introductory course.
The study analyzed data from 75 students—40 CS majors and 35 non-CS majors—all freshmen without prior programming experience. The researchers found that students' backgrounds and areas of study can affect how they approach and progress in learning programming.
Key Suggestions from the Research
- Differentiated teaching strategies: Offer tailored support based on students' academic backgrounds
- Early identification of at-risk students: Use early diagnostic assessments to identify students who may need additional support
- Peer learning opportunities: Pair students from different majors to leverage diverse problem-solving approaches
Practical Implications for Faculty
1. Scaffold Early Assignments
Break complex programming tasks into smaller, manageable steps with clear instructions and examples.
2. Provide Multiple Representations
Use flowcharts, pseudocode, and visual programming tools alongside traditional code to help students build mental models.
3. Create Relevance
Connect programming concepts to students' own disciplines—biology students analyzing data, business students building dashboards.
4. Normalize Struggle
Explicitly tell students that programming is challenging for everyone at first, and that persistence is more important than prior experience.
5. Offer Multiple Pathways to Success
Consider alternative assessments that allow students to demonstrate understanding in different ways.

title: A Map Makes You Smarter, GPS Does Not: AI, Work, and What Comes Next
summary: Jose Antonio Bowen's workshop on AI assignments and assessments explores how AI is reshaping entry-level work and what it means for how we teach.
content_plain:
Jose Antonio Bowen's workshop on AI assignments and assessments explores how AI is reshaping entry-level work and what it means for how we teach. Jose Antonio Bowen, author of Teaching Naked, Teaching Change, and Teaching with AI, provided a workshop on AI Assignment and Assessments where he mentioned:
"A map makes you smarter. GPS does not."
This wasn't just about directions. It was about how we're all starting to think less, remember less, and—if we're not careful—become less, all thanks to the technology we depend on.
The Decline of Entry-Level Everything
AI is not just automating factory floors—it's hollowing out the very starting points of professional careers. Junior developers, entry-level analysts, first-year associates—these roles are being compressed or eliminated. The traditional career ladder assumed you'd learn by doing the grunt work first. But if AI does the grunt work, where does the learning happen?
The New Skill Stack
Bowen argues that the skills that matter most now are:
- Critical thinking — not just analyzing, but knowing what to analyze
- Communication — the ability to frame problems and explain solutions to humans
- Creativity — generating novel approaches, not just optimizing existing ones
- Collaboration — working across disciplines and with AI systems
Implications for Course Design
1. Design assignments that AI can't do alone — require personal reflection, local context, or original data collection
2. Make the process visible — require drafts, documentation of reasoning, and revision histories
3. Teach students to evaluate AI output — critical assessment of AI-generated content is itself a higher-order skill
4. Emphasize oral communication — presentations, debates, and verbal explanations resist AI substitution
The Map Metaphor
A map requires you to orient yourself, make decisions, and build a mental model of the terrain. GPS removes all of that cognitive work. The same is true for AI in education: if we let it do the thinking, students never build the mental maps they need.
The goal isn't to ban AI—it's to ensure students are building maps, not just following GPS.

title: Posit Connect and Shiny for Teaching and Research
summary: An overview of how IU uses Shiny and Posit Connect to create interactive data visualization tools for teaching statistics and sharing research.
content_plain:
An overview of how IU uses Shiny and Posit Connect to create interactive data visualization tools for teaching statistics and sharing research. Overview of Shiny & Posit Connect
Shiny is a package in R (from Posit, formerly RStudio) that allows users to build interactive web applications using R. It's widely used for creating data visualizations and tools without needing web development skills.
IU uses Posit Connect (formerly RStudio Connect) to host and share Shiny applications.
Why Use Shiny?
- Easy for R users to create apps for statistical analysis, data science, and teaching
- Offers interactive elements like sliders and panels, which update visualizations in real time
- Outputs render in browsers like standard web apps
Posit Connect at IU
- IU has a central Posit Connect server hosting apps for research and teaching
- Teaching apps include interactive tools for statistics classes (e.g., manipulating effect size and observing graph changes)
- Research apps are used to share data tools and visualizations with broader communities
Hosting Details
- Free Posit-hosted options are available for students and small-scale apps
- IU opted for local licensing due to its in-house technical expertise
- Faculty can request access through IU's research computing services
Getting Started
1. Install R and RStudio
2. Install the Shiny package
3. Build your app locally
4. Deploy to Posit Connect for sharing with students or collaborators

title: Bridging the Gap: What Tech Practitioners Really Want from CS Education
summary: Over 1,000 computing practitioners shared what they think CS graduates need—more coursework, better soft skills, and real-world project experience.
content_plain:
Over 1,000 computing practitioners shared what they think CS graduates need—more coursework, better soft skills, and real-world project experience. In the spring of 2024, the Computing Research Association (CRA) asked: What do industry professionals think about the way we teach computer science today?
More than 1,000 experienced computing practitioners—most with over two decades of experience—shared their thoughts on how well today's CS graduates are being prepared for the real world.
The Call for More—and Better—Coursework
One of the loudest messages: students need more coursework in core CS subjects. Respondents recommended about four additional CS courses beyond what's typical today. Algorithms, computer architecture, and theoretical foundations topped the list.
Soft Skills Matter More Than You Think
Practitioners consistently highlighted the need for better communication, teamwork, and project management skills. These aren't "nice-to-haves"—they're essential for professional success.
Real-World Experience is Non-Negotiable
Internships, co-ops, and project-based courses were strongly recommended. Practitioners want graduates who have worked on real codebases, collaborated in teams, and dealt with ambiguity.
AI and Emerging Technologies
Respondents emphasized the importance of AI literacy—not just using AI tools, but understanding their foundations, limitations, and ethical implications.
Key Takeaways for Faculty
1. Strengthen core CS foundations — don't sacrifice depth for breadth
2. Integrate communication and teamwork into technical courses
3. Create authentic project experiences that mirror industry workflows
4. Teach AI critically — as both a tool and a subject of study
5. Build industry connections through guest speakers, advisory boards, and partnerships

title: Mid-Semester Feedback and Google Illuminate for Faculty
summary: Using Google Illuminate to create podcast-style audio summaries of research, plus practical tips for designing and analyzing mid-semester feedback surveys.
content_plain:
Using Google Illuminate to create podcast-style audio summaries of research, plus practical tips for designing and analyzing mid-semester feedback surveys. It's a great time to think about receiving midsemester feedback on your course. This time, a new resource might be helpful: Google Illuminate.
What is Google Illuminate?
Google Illuminate is an AI tool that creates podcast-style audio summaries of research papers. It's designed to make complex academic information more accessible and engaging.
How It Works
1. Input a research paper, PDF, or URL into Illuminate
2. Illuminate generates an audio summary explaining key points
3. You can ask questions about the document
4. Convert text output into a podcast answer
Note: Do NOT input copyrighted information. However, it is optimized for computer science topics and supports research papers hosted on arXiv.org.
Mid-Semester Feedback Best Practices
Why Collect Mid-Semester Feedback?
- Identify what's working and what needs adjustment
- Show students their input matters
- Make evidence-based changes while there's still time
Tips for Effective Surveys
- Keep surveys short (5-10 questions max)
- Mix quantitative and qualitative questions
- Ask about specific, actionable aspects of the course
- Share results and planned changes with students
Sample Questions
- What is helping your learning in this class?
- What is hindering your learning?
- What could the instructor change to improve your experience?
- How effective are the in-class activities for your learning?
Analyzing Feedback
1. Look for patterns, not individual outliers
2. Identify 2-3 actionable changes
3. Communicate changes to students
4. Follow up later to see if changes helped

title: Classroom Assessment Techniques: Formative Feedback Tools
summary: The updated 2024 edition of the classic CATs handbook offers practical, research-based tools for checking student understanding in any teaching format.
content_plain:
The updated 2024 edition of the classic CATs handbook offers practical, research-based tools for checking student understanding in any teaching format. A new (2024) version of the classic book, Classroom Assessment Techniques: Formative Feedback Tools for College and University Teachers, is available in the IU Library.
Classroom Assessment Techniques (CATs) are simple, low-pressure ways to check how well students are understanding the material. These methods are efficient, student-centered strategies that provide valuable insights into learning progress.
How CATs Enhance Learning
- Focus Students' Attention: Starting with a quick CAT activity can help students focus and prepare to engage
- Spot Trouble Early: A simple check-in can reveal concepts that need more explanation
Categories of CATs
1. Knowledge Recall & Understanding
- Empty Outline: Provide a partially completed outline; students fill in key concepts
- One-Sentence Summary: Students summarize a topic in one structured sentence
2. Analysis & Critical Thinking
- Categorization Grid: Students sort concepts into categories
- Pro-Con Grid: Students list arguments for and against a position
3. Synthesis & Creative Thinking
- Concept Map: Students draw connections between ideas
- Invented Dialogues: Students write conversations between theorists or concepts
4. Problem-Solving
- Documented Problem Solution: Students show their work AND explain their reasoning
- What's the Principle? Students identify which principle applies to a given problem
5. Application & Performance
- Directed Paraphrasing: Students explain a concept to a specific audience
- Application Cards: After learning a concept, students write one real-world application
6. Learner Attitudes & Self-Awareness
- Classroom Opinion Polls: Quick surveys on course-related topics
- Self-Confidence Surveys: Students rate their confidence on specific skills
Getting Started
Choose ONE technique. Try it in your next class. Reflect on what you learned about your students' understanding. Adjust your teaching accordingly.

title: Instructor Talk: The Power of Non-Content Language in the Classroom
summary: Research shows that instructor talk—language not directly about course content—plays a crucial role in shaping classroom environments and student engagement.
content_plain:
Research shows that instructor talk—language not directly about course content—plays a crucial role in shaping classroom environments and student engagement. Studies by Seidel et al. (2015) and Harrison et al. (2019) have demonstrated how Instructor Talk plays a crucial role in shaping classroom environments, influencing student engagement, learning attitudes, and potentially mitigating stereotype threats.
Instructor talk is defined as any language used by an instructor that is not directly related to course content but instead shapes the learning environment.
Five Major Categories of Non-Content Talk
1. Building the Instructor/Student Relationship — Encouraging respect, boosting self-efficacy, and offering advice for student success
2. Establishing Classroom Culture — Setting expectations, fostering a sense of community
3. Explaining Pedagogical Choices — Justifying teaching methods to help students understand why certain approaches are used
4. Sharing Personal Experiences — Providing personal anecdotes or relating to student experiences
