5. Unmasking Science — Discussing the nature of science and emphasizing diversity within the field
Harrison et al. (2019) added: "Negatively Phrased Instructor Talk" — statements that may discourage students or undermine confidence.
Examples of Positively Phrased Instructor Talk
Building Relationships
- "I know this material can be challenging, but I'm here to help you succeed."
- "My office hours are a great place to work through problems together."
Establishing Culture
- "In this class, mistakes are part of the learning process."
- "Everyone's perspective adds value to our discussions."
Explaining Pedagogy
- "We're doing peer review because research shows it deepens understanding."
- "I use live coding so you can see how I think through problems."
Sharing Experiences
- "When I was learning this, I struggled with the same concept."
- "In industry, I saw this principle applied in unexpected ways."
Practical Tips
- Be intentional about your non-content language
- Record yourself and listen for patterns
- Ask a colleague to observe and provide feedback
- Replace negative framings with growth-oriented language
The way we talk to students—beyond the content—shapes their experience, confidence, and sense of belonging.

title: Quick Tip: Wise Feedback
summary: Wise feedback combines high standards with high support—communicating that you believe in a student's ability to meet expectations while giving concrete direction.
content_plain:
Wise feedback combines high standards with high support—communicating that you believe in a student's ability to meet expectations while giving concrete direction. The article "Breaking the Cycle of Mistrust: Wise Interventions to Provide Critical Feedback Across the Racial Divide" by Yeager et al. introduces the concept of wise feedback—a strategy that helps instructors frame feedback in a way that communicates high expectations AND belief in the student's ability to meet them.
What is Wise Feedback?
Wise feedback has three components:
1. High standards: "I'm giving you these comments because I have high standards."
2. Assurance of capability: "And I believe you can meet them."
3. Specific direction: "Here's what to focus on to improve."
Why It Works
Research shows that wise feedback:
- Increases student motivation and revision effort
- Reduces the trust gap that can exist between instructors and students from underrepresented groups
- Helps students see critical feedback as an investment, not a judgment
Examples for Computing Courses
Code Review
"I'm pushing back on your implementation because I know you can write cleaner, more efficient code. Specifically, look at how you're handling the loop on line 23—there's a more elegant approach using list comprehension."
Project Feedback
"This project shows strong conceptual understanding. I'm flagging the database design because, with your skills, you can create something more normalized and scalable. Here's where to start..."
Written Work
"Your analysis of the algorithm's complexity is on the right track. I'm asking you to revise because I think you can articulate the reasoning more precisely. Focus on explaining why the recurrence relation simplifies the way it does."
Implementation Tips
- Add a wise feedback statement to your rubric template
- Use it consistently across all students
- Combine with specific, actionable suggestions
- Follow up to see if students engaged with the feedback

title: Evidence-Based Research Supporting the Use of PlayPosit in Classes
summary: PlayPosit turns passive videos into interactive mini-lessons with embedded questions, feedback, and polling—supported by research on formative assessment and active learning.
content_plain:
PlayPosit turns passive videos into interactive mini-lessons with embedded questions, feedback, and polling—supported by research on formative assessment and active learning. PlayPosit is an interactive teaching tool used to make interactive videos, also known as bulbs. The application can be integrated into Canvas using your own videos or by extracting videos from other sources, such as YouTube or TED Talks.
Educators can convert videos into interactive, topic- and student-focused mini-lessons by adding questions at appropriate time-points. PlayPosit videos pause at intervals chosen by the instructor to give students an opportunity to respond.
Key Features
- Multiple playback options: Allow students to rewind, fast forward, or retake activities
- Instant feedback: Students see whether they answered correctly and understand the rationale
- Formative assessment: Designed to guide the teaching process, not as outcome indicators
Research Support
Active Learning & Engagement
Research consistently shows that interactive video increases student engagement. When students must respond to questions during a video, they maintain attention and process information more deeply.
Formative Assessment Benefits
PlayPosit enables instructors to modify instructional activities midstream based on their effectiveness. Because formative evaluations are under the control of the instructor, they can target specific instructional topics.
The Testing Effect
Embedding questions in video leverages the testing effect—students who answer questions during learning retain more information than those who passively watch.
Getting Started
1. Access PlayPosit through your institution's teaching tools
2. Select or upload a video
3. Add interaction points (questions, discussions, polls)
4. Configure playback and feedback settings
5. Integrate into your Canvas course

title: Integrating Metacognitive and Student Engagement Strategies into Your Practice
summary: How K-12 reforms and COVID-19 have shaped college students' learning habits, and what faculty can do to build metacognitive skills and engagement.
content_plain:
How K-12 reforms and COVID-19 have shaped college students' learning habits, and what faculty can do to build metacognitive skills and engagement. The article "How Changes in K-12 Schooling Hampered the Preparation of College Students," published in the Chronicle of Higher Education, explores how reforms in K-12 education, combined with the COVID-19 pandemic and teacher shortages, have affected the academic habits of today's college students.
The Challenge
Test-based accountability policies like "No Child Left Behind" prioritized short-term performance over long-term learning. Teaching became narrowly focused on test-taking skills, leaving students unprepared for broader academic tasks.
When students arrive at college, they are conditioned to expect that same level of guidance. They struggle with open-ended assignments, self-directed learning, and ambiguity.
Metacognitive Strategies for Faculty
1. Make Learning Processes Visible
- Explain why you're using specific teaching methods
- Model your own thinking process when solving problems
- Ask students to reflect on their learning strategies
2. Teach Study Skills Explicitly
- Show students how to read technical documentation
- Demonstrate effective debugging strategies
- Share time management and project planning techniques
3. Use Wrapper Activities
- Before an exam: "How did you prepare? What strategies did you use?"
- After an exam: "How did your preparation match the actual questions?"
- Around assignments: "What was the hardest part? What would you do differently?"
Engagement Strategies
Create Low-Stakes Practice Opportunities
- Frequent quizzes with immediate feedback
- In-class exercises that build toward larger projects
- Peer review sessions
Build Student Agency
- Offer choices in assignments when possible
- Let students set personal learning goals
- Encourage self-assessment before submission
Foster a Growth Mindset
- Normalize struggle and iteration
- Celebrate improvement, not just achievement
- Share stories of professional learning curves
The goal is to help students become independent, self-regulated learners who can thrive beyond our classrooms.

title: Creating Inclusive Course Materials
summary: Practical strategies for making course materials more inclusive—from open educational resources and diverse representations to accessible design and UDL principles.
content_plain:
Practical strategies for making course materials more inclusive—from open educational resources and diverse representations to accessible design and UDL principles. This resource is adapted from Inclusive Education and UDL: A Comprehensive Guide to include resources available at IU's campus.
Open Course Materials
Expanding course materials to reflect diversity in source and opinion prepares students for their personal and professional lives. The cost of required textbooks and technology can be a huge burden for students. Consider:
- See if relevant content can be found free online via Open Educational Resources
- Make content accessible through the LMS regardless of geographical and time constraints
- Develop a full understanding of fair use and copyright policies
IU Resources
- IU Libraries: Course reserves, interlibrary loan, and subject librarians can help identify free or low-cost alternatives
- Canvas Commons: Shared learning objects and modules from IU instructors
- UITS Teaching Tools: Technology tools available at no cost to IU instructors
Diverse Representation in Materials
- Include examples, case studies, and readings from diverse authors and perspectives
- Use inclusive language in all course documents
- Represent diverse identities in visual materials and scenarios
- Connect content to a variety of cultural contexts
Accessible Design Principles
- Use heading structures in all documents
- Provide alt text for images
- Ensure sufficient color contrast
- Caption all videos and provide transcripts
- Use accessible file formats (avoid image-only PDFs)
Universal Design for Learning (UDL)
UDL provides a framework for creating flexible learning experiences:
- Multiple means of engagement: Offer choices, connect to interests, foster collaboration
- Multiple means of representation: Present information in multiple formats
- Multiple means of action and expression: Allow varied ways to demonstrate learning

title: Teaching and Learning Conversation: Decoding the Disciplines with David Pace
summary: Extended notes from a conversation on the Decoding the Disciplines framework—a systematic approach to identifying and addressing bottlenecks in student learning.
content_plain:
Extended notes from a conversation on the Decoding the Disciplines framework—a systematic approach to identifying and addressing bottlenecks in student learning. Decoding student learning is a framework for understanding where students are struggling and how to address those struggles systematically.
What is Decoding Student Learning?
Decoding student learning is a strategy for identifying the bottlenecks in student learning and developing targeted interventions to address those bottlenecks.
The 7 Questions of Decoding Student Learning
1. What is the bottleneck? — Where do students consistently struggle?
2. What does an expert do? — How does a professional in the field approach this task?
3. How can the expert process be made visible? — Can you model your thinking explicitly?
4. How can students practice? — What activities let students rehearse the expert process?
5. What motivates students? — How can you help students see the value?
6. How well are students mastering the process? — What evidence of learning can you collect?
7. How can you share what you've learned? — How can insights be disseminated?
Applying Decoding in Computing Courses
Example Bottleneck: Students Can't Debug Effectively
1. Bottleneck: Students stare at error messages without a strategy
2. Expert process: Experienced developers read the error, form a hypothesis, isolate the problem, test the fix
3. Making it visible: Live debugging demonstrations with think-alouds
4. Practice: Give students buggy code with structured debugging worksheets
5. Motivation: Connect debugging to professional practice and interview skills
6. Assessment: Observe debugging process, not just the final fix
7. Sharing: Document successful interventions for colleagues
Key Takeaways
- Bottlenecks are often invisible to experts because the process has become automatic
- Making expert thinking visible is the most powerful intervention
- Students need structured practice with feedback
- The framework works across disciplines and course levels

title: Student Engagement Roster (SER) Reminder
summary: How to use IU's Student Engagement Roster to provide timely feedback, flag at-risk students, and support academic advisors in helping students succeed.
content_plain:
How to use IU's Student Engagement Roster to provide timely feedback, flag at-risk students, and support academic advisors in helping students succeed. It's the time of year to discuss the importance of using the Student Engagement Roster (SERs) to provide students with timely feedback on how they are doing in your course.
These reports also provide records of your observations and recommendations to academic advisors, who can look for patterns across all a student's courses and reach out in more targeted and supportive ways.
Key Dates and Actions
- Week 2: Report on student attendance to meet IUB's Attendance Verification requirement
- Week 3: Consider giving students a diagnostic task to identify students who may be struggling
- Weeks 4-6: Submit early feedback through SER, especially for students showing signs of difficulty
What to Report
- Attendance patterns
- Assignment completion
- Class participation and engagement
- Academic performance concerns
- Positive progress and strengths
Why It Matters
Early intervention is one of the most effective strategies for student retention and success. When advisors can see patterns across multiple courses, they can provide more comprehensive support.
Tips for Effective SER Use
1. Be specific in your comments—vague feedback is less actionable
2. Note both concerns AND strengths
3. Submit reports promptly so advisors can act quickly
4. Follow up with students directly when possible
5. Use SER data to inform your own instructional adjustments

title: Sample Questions for Anonymous and Mid-Semester Feedback Surveys
summary: A curated collection of survey questions organized by category—from general feedback to inclusive teaching, pacing, and content—to help you gather targeted student input.
content_plain:
A curated collection of survey questions organized by category—from general feedback to inclusive teaching, pacing, and content—to help you gather targeted student input. Mid-semester feedback lets instructors "check the pulse" of their class partway through the semester. By surveying students, especially after a chance to evaluate evidence of learning, instructors and students alike can gain practical and actionable insights.
General Questions
- What is helping your learning in this class?
- What is hindering your learning in this class?
- What could the instructor change to improve your learning experience?
Course Goals & Structure
- How clear are the course learning objectives to you?
- Do you understand how assignments connect to the overall goals of the course?
- Is the workload appropriate for a course at this level?
Inclusive Teaching Practice
- Do you feel comfortable asking questions or sharing ideas in class?
- Do you feel that all students are treated with respect?
- Are diverse perspectives and examples included in the course materials?
Classroom Dynamics
- How well do group activities support your learning?
- Do you feel that class discussions are productive?
- Is there sufficient opportunity for questions during class?
Pacing & Content
- Is the pace of the course too fast, too slow, or about right?
- Are there specific topics that need more time or explanation?
- How effective are the assigned readings/videos in preparing you for class?
Assessment & Feedback
- Is the feedback you receive on assignments helpful for improving?
- Are the grading criteria clear and fair?
- Do you understand how your grade is determined?
Tips for Using These Questions
1. Select 5-10 questions most relevant to your course
2. Keep the survey anonymous to encourage honest responses
3. Share results with students and describe any changes you'll make
4. Follow up later in the semester to see if changes helped
5. Don't try to address everything — pick 2-3 actionable items

title: What Does It Mean to Understand Your Learners?
summary: Reflection prompts and actionable strategies to help faculty understand how their students learn, what motivates them, and how to customize teaching accordingly.
content_plain:
Reflection prompts and actionable strategies to help faculty understand how their students learn, what motivates them, and how to customize teaching accordingly. Better understanding the students we teach provides us with an advantage: we can customize our teaching in ways that connect with their motivations and maximize their learning.
While it's not always possible to adapt learning materials to each student's specific preferences, it is helpful to know what they think works best for them and to translate that into actions they can take to improve their learning.
Reflection Prompts for Students
1. Preferred Learning Modalities
- Do you find it easier to learn through visual aids, verbal instruction, or hands-on work?
- Which types of in-class activities help you learn best?
- How do you feel about class participation and discussions?
- Take Action: Experiment with different modalities to discover what works best for you.
2. Learning Environment
- Do you prefer a structured environment with clear guidelines, or a more flexible approach?
- How does the physical classroom setup affect your ability to focus?
- Take Action: Identify your ideal study environment and seek it out.
3. Prior Knowledge and Experience
- What do you already know about the subject?
- Have you had previous courses or experiences related to this topic?
- Take Action: Connect new material to what you already know.
4. Motivation and Goals
- Why are you taking this course?
- What do you hope to gain from it professionally or personally?
- Take Action: Set specific, achievable goals for the course.
5. Challenges and Support Needs
- What challenges do you anticipate in this course?
- What types of support are most helpful for you?
- Take Action: Identify campus resources and reach out early.
For Faculty
Use these prompts in a first-week survey to better understand your students. Even simple knowledge of their backgrounds, motivations, and concerns can help you make more informed instructional decisions.

title: Resources for AI Syllabus Policy Statements
summary: A collection of resources and examples for crafting clear AI use policies in your syllabus—from full prohibition to guided integration.
content_plain:
A collection of resources and examples for crafting clear AI use policies in your syllabus—from full prohibition to guided integration. IU does not currently have any specific policies around the use of generative AI, other than policies related to data security and privacy.
This means the decision about how AI tools are used in your course is up to you as the instructor. Having a clear policy in your syllabus helps set expectations and reduces ambiguity for students.
Spectrum of AI Policies
1. No AI Use Permitted
"The use of generative AI tools is not permitted for any assignments in this course. All work must be entirely your own."
2. AI as a Learning Tool (with disclosure)
"You may use AI tools for brainstorming and research, but all submitted work must be written by you. If you use AI at any stage, you must disclose how it was used."
3. AI Integrated with Guidelines
"AI tools are permitted and encouraged for specific tasks. For each assignment, I will specify which AI uses are acceptable. You must document your AI interactions."
4. Open AI Use
"You are free to use AI tools as you see fit. However, you are responsible for the accuracy and quality of your final submission."
Key Elements of an AI Policy
- Be specific: Which tools? Which assignments? What constitutes acceptable use?
- Explain the rationale: Help students understand why the policy exists
- Address citation: How should students acknowledge AI use?
- Consider equity: Not all students have equal access to AI tools
- Plan for evolution: AI capabilities change rapidly; build in flexibility
Resources
- Stanford's guidance on AI in education
- George Mason University's strategies for teaching with AI
- IU's data security and privacy policies for AI tools
- TeachAI toolkit for educational institutions

title: The Liquid Syllabus: A Humanizing First Impression
summary: A liquid syllabus is a public, mobile-friendly webpage that welcomes students with warmth, clarity, and support—ensuring they start the course feeling connected.
content_plain:
A liquid syllabus is a public, mobile-friendly webpage that welcomes students with warmth, clarity, and support—ensuring they start the course feeling connected. A Liquid Syllabus (Pacansky-Brock, 2021) is a humanizing element that ensures students start a course feeling supported by their instructor. Rather than a PDF locked inside a learning management system, it is a public, accessible, mobile-friendly website that opens instantly and renders beautifully on a phone.
The Components
1. Welcome Video
A brief, imperfect welcome video at the top. It doesn't need to be polished—authenticity matters more than production quality.
2. Learning Pact
Articulates what students can expect from their instructor and what will be expected of them. Written in supportive, hopeful language.
3. Week One Due Dates
A clear list of what students need to do in the first week, reducing anxiety and confusion.
4. Tips for Success
Practical advice written in asset-based language that encourages growth and recognizes the experiences students bring to class.
5. Policies (Optional)
If included, policies are written in welcoming language rather than legalistic tone.
Why It Works
- Reduces barriers: Students can access it on any device without logging in
- Builds connection: A welcome video creates a human connection before class begins
- Sets tone: Supportive language signals that the instructor cares about student success
- Improves equity: Ensures all students, including those unfamiliar with university systems, get essential information early
How to Create One
1. Use Google Sites (free, easy to use, mobile-friendly)
2. Record a brief welcome video (2-3 minutes)
3. Write your learning pact and success tips
4. List week one essentials
5. Email the link to students the week before classes start
Key Principles
- Use asset-based, growth-oriented language
- Keep it simple and scannable
- Make it personal—your personality should come through
- Update it each semester with fresh content

title: Updating Canvas for the Start of the Semester
summary: A comprehensive checklist to help your Canvas course meet Quality Matters standards with proper settings, organized content, and accessible design.
content_plain:
A comprehensive checklist to help your Canvas course meet Quality Matters standards with proper settings, organized content, and accessible design. This checklist helps your course meet the minimum expectations of Quality Matters with proper settings and organized content.
Canvas Setup
- Import from previous semesters: Make sure your course content has been successfully imported
- Update dates: Adjust all assignment due dates, availability dates, and event dates
- Check links: Verify all external links are still working
- Review modules: Organize content in a logical, sequential structure
Course Settings
- Home page: Set an informative home page (consider a module or assignment list view)
- Navigation: Customize the navigation menu to show only what students need
- Notifications: Configure default notification preferences
- Course status: Keep the course unpublished until you're ready
Content Organization
- Consistent naming: Use a clear naming convention for modules and assignments
- Module structure: Group content by week or topic with clear titles
- Front-load essentials: Put the most important information where students will see it first
- Syllabus: Upload or link your syllabus in an easily findable location
Accessibility
- Alt text: Add descriptive alt text to all images
- Headings: Use proper heading hierarchy in pages
- Color contrast: Ensure text is readable against backgrounds
- File formats: Provide accessible versions of all documents
- Captions: Ensure videos have accurate captions
Communication
- Welcome announcement: Draft a welcoming first announcement
- Office hours: Post your office hours and how to schedule meetings
- Contact information: Make it easy for students to reach you

title: Integrating Retrieval Practice into Classroom Instruction
summary: Retrieval practice—having students recall information from memory—is one of the most effective learning strategies. Here's how to integrate it into your teaching.
content_plain:
Retrieval practice—having students recall information from memory—is one of the most effective learning strategies. Here's how to integrate it into your teaching. Retrieval practice is one of the most effective methods of learning and remembering information for long-term use. During retrieval practice, students "retrieve" what they know about a topic out of their memory.
Retrieval practice requires effort on the part of the student to recall specific information, which is beneficial to improving learning and strengthening memory. It can be a no-stakes learning opportunity that increases student performance beyond formative and summative assessments.
Effective Learning Strategies
Spaced Practice
Distribute study over time rather than cramming. Review material at increasing intervals.
Classroom Application: Schedule brief review activities throughout the semester, not just before exams.
Interleaving
Mix different topics or types of problems during practice rather than focusing on one type at a time.
Classroom Application: Design problem sets that mix concepts from multiple weeks.
Retrieval Practice
Practice bringing information to mind without looking at notes.
Classroom Application: Begin class with a brief, no-stakes recall activity. Use "brain dumps" where students write everything they remember about a topic.
Elaboration
Connect new information to prior knowledge by explaining how and why things work.
Classroom Application: Ask students to explain concepts in their own words or teach them to a partner.
Implementation Tips
1. Start small: Add one retrieval activity per class session
2. Make it low-stakes: The goal is learning, not grading
3. Provide feedback: Let students check their recall against correct answers
4. Be consistent: Regular practice builds the habit
5. Explain the science: Help students understand why retrieval practice works

title: Active Learning in Computer Science
summary: Active learning approaches in CS—from jigsaw methods to peer instruction—get students involved in doing rather than just listening, with proven results.
content_plain:
Active learning approaches in CS—from jigsaw methods to peer instruction—get students involved in doing rather than just listening, with proven results. Active learning is defined as approaches that get students involved in activity in the classroom rather than passively listening to a lecture. Students who engage in active learning understand the "why" behind a subject versus merely trying to memorize it.
Methods for CS Classrooms
Jigsaw Method
Students learn parts of the material in small groups, then regroup so each new group has one expert on each piece. This method was successfully used in an Introduction to Computer Architecture course.
Peer Instruction
Pose a conceptual question, let students think individually, then discuss with neighbors before responding again. Research shows this improves conceptual understanding significantly.
Think-Pair-Share
Students think about a problem individually, discuss with a partner, then share with the class. Simple to implement and effective for any class size.
Live Coding
Demonstrate coding in real-time, making mistakes and debugging along the way. Students see the problem-solving process, not just the final product.
Code Reviews
Students review each other's code using structured criteria. Builds critical analysis skills and exposes students to different approaches.
Pair Programming
Two students work at one computer—one types (driver) while the other reviews (navigator). Roles switch regularly.
Why It Matters in CS
A meta-analysis of 225 studies found that active learning environments improved exam scores by 6% and reduced failure rates by 1.5x (Freeman et al., 2014). In CS specifically, active learning helps students develop debugging strategies, design thinking, and collaborative skills that passive lectures cannot.
Getting Started
1. Pick ONE technique to try in your next class
2. Start with a small, low-stakes activity (5-10 minutes)
3. Debrief with students—ask what worked
4. Gradually expand as you gain confidence

title: Trying Something New in Your Course
summary: A practical framework for experimenting with new teaching approaches—from improving what you already do to measuring impact and sharing what you learn.
content_plain:
A practical framework for experimenting with new teaching approaches—from improving what you already do to measuring impact and sharing what you learn. Here are a few ideas to consider when trying something new, adapted from Tips for Teachers.
Can I Improve Something I'm Already Doing?
Before making wholesale changes, identify practices you already do and look for ways to improve them. This takes less time and effort and gives you a platform of success.
Example: Instead of several worked examples you rush through, choose one or two that you've thought carefully about. Spend time going through them. Consider modeling them in silence first, then using carefully considered self-explanation prompts.
How Will I Know If the Idea Works?
How are you going to know if the idea has been a success? The more objective the measure, the better.
- Compare student performance on similar assessments before and after the change
- Collect student feedback through surveys or focus groups
- Track engagement metrics (attendance, participation, assignment completion)
- Keep a teaching journal noting your observations
Start Small
Don't try to overhaul everything at once. Pick one thing to change and give it time to take effect.
- Try the new approach in one section first
- Give it at least 3-4 weeks before evaluating
- Be transparent with students about what you're trying and why
Document and Share
- Keep notes on what you tried, what happened, and what you'd do differently
- Consider writing about your experience for a teaching journal
- Share with colleagues at department meetings or teaching workshops
Resources for Innovation
- Tips for Teachers by Craig Barton
- IU's Center for Innovative Teaching and Learning
- Teaching-focused journals in your discipline
- Peer observation and feedback programs

title: End of the Semester Tips
summary: Practical ideas for wrapping up the semester—from preserving your teaching portfolio to reflecting on what worked and planning revisions for next time.
content_plain:
Practical ideas for wrapping up the semester—from preserving your teaching portfolio to reflecting on what worked and planning revisions for next time. The following are ideas adapted from the SUNY Teaching and Learning Center that may help you prepare for next semester and update your teaching portfolio.
Keep a Copy of Everything
Your teaching portfolio may include syllabi, assignments, and assessments you've designed. Make sure you keep copies so you have options for your portfolio. As the semester ends, make small notes about the genesis of certain themes or assessments—these notes may prove useful later.
Is There an Article?
Think about the larger impact of practices from your courses. Did you try something new? Reflect on your pedagogical practices and what new learning experiences they opened up. Is there something you want to write about and share with other instructors?
Several journals welcome articles about pedagogical practices, including the International Journal of Designs for Learning.
Think About Revision
Even if you don't think you'll teach this course next semester, take a few minutes to think about:
- What to keep — What worked well this semester?
- What to revise and keep — What has potential but needs adjustment?
- What to toss — What didn't serve students or your goals?
Gather Student Feedback
Beyond formal course evaluations, consider:
- What themes emerged in mid-semester feedback?
- What questions did students ask most frequently?
- Where did students struggle most?
Take Care of Yourself
- Set boundaries for end-of-semester grading
- Celebrate what went well
- Rest and recharge before planning for next semester
- Connect with colleagues for support and perspective

title: Joy-Centered Teaching: Essential Reads for Faculty Book Clubs
summary: A curated collection of books on joyful, care-centered, and hope-driven pedagogy—compiled by faculty educators committed to teaching as an act of human flourishing.
content_plain:
A curated collection of books on joyful, care-centered, and hope-driven pedagogy—compiled by faculty educators committed to teaching as an act of human flourishing. In recent months, faculty across higher education have been grappling with unprecedented challenges: from navigating rapid technological changes and AI integration to addressing student mental health concerns and institutional pressures that often feel overwhelming. Yet amid these difficulties, educators are increasingly recognizing that joy, care, and hope aren't luxuries in teaching—they're essential to sustainable, transformative pedagogy.
This spring, as we move through another semester of complex demands, one instructional designer reached out to a vibrant community of faculty educators on a teaching and learning mailing list, seeking uplifting book recommendations for a faculty book club focused on joyful teaching. The response was inspiring.
Essential Reads for Joy-Centered Teaching
Joy-Centered Pedagogy in Higher Education
Lori Gonzalez, Editor (Open Access)
This collaborative collection stands out not only for its accessibility—it's completely open-access—but for its practical wisdom. Editor Lori Gonzalez has structured the volume with "reader reflection" questions at the end of each chapter and a compilation of teaching tips that serve as perfect discussion fodder for book clubs.
A Pedagogy of Kindness
Catherine Denial
This book emerged repeatedly in the recommendations, with one educator noting they "can't recommend it highly enough." Another shared that their Early Career Learning Community recently completed the book, which "prompted us to challenge our assumptions about 'rigor' and 'good teaching' and to rethink our classroom practices to foster meaningful learning."
Hope Circuits: Re-wiring Universities for Human Flourishing
Lee Skallerup Bessette (McGill–Queen's University Press, 2024)
Written precisely for educators "trying to do hopeful, joyful, justice-oriented work inside institutions that often feel like they're fraying," Bessette's book tackles the gap between our aspirations for higher education and institutional realities.
The Caring University
Kevin McClure
McClure provides a research-informed account of how institutions can center care—not as empty rhetoric, but as "a set of practices, structures, and leadership habits that make joy and belonging possible for students, staff, and faculty."
The Spark of Learning
Sarah Rose Cavanagh
Cavanagh's central argument is elegantly simple yet profound: emotions are contagious, and "conveying passion, enthusiasm, and love for learning in the classroom is the best route to motivating student engagement and thus learning."
The Present Professor
Elizabeth A. Norell
If we want to create spaces where students have the confidence, comfort, and psychological safety to learn and grow, we have to create spaces where we do, too. The Present Professor is dedicated to that proposition.
Why These Books Matter Now
What emerges from this rich collection is a vision of teaching that refuses to choose between rigor and care, between justice and joy, between individual practice and institutional transformation. These authors collectively argue that in times of crisis and complexity, joy isn't a distraction from serious pedagogical work—it's essential to it.
The timing couldn't be better to gather with colleagues, pour some coffee or tea, and explore together what it means to teach joyfully in challenging times.
This list was compiled from recommendations shared by faculty educators on a mailing list dedicated to teaching and learning in higher education.

title: TeachAI: Resources for AI in Education
summary: TeachAI is a toolkit designed to help education leaders integrate AI responsibly—with seven guiding principles and practical strategies for classroom use.
content_plain:
TeachAI is a toolkit designed to help education leaders integrate AI responsibly—with seven guiding principles and practical strategies for classroom use. TeachAI is an educational resource designed to help education leaders realize the potential benefits of AI while addressing the risks. While primarily aimed at K-12 educators, it integrates resources specific to higher education.
The Seven Principles
1. Purpose: Use AI to help all students achieve educational goals
2. Compliance: Reaffirm adherence to existing policies
3. Knowledge: Promote AI literacy
4. Balance: Realize the benefits of AI and address the risks
5. Integrity: Advance academic integrity
6. Agency: Maintain human decision-making when using AI
7. Evaluation: Regularly assess the impacts of AI
Key Goals
- Guidance Leads to Transformation: Policies coupled with organizational learning can set the stage for improvement across the system
- Empowering Educators: Teachers and faculty should be at the center of AI integration decisions
- Student-Centered Approach: AI use should ultimately serve student learning outcomes
TeachAI Toolkit Features
- Vision statement templates for institutional AI policies
- Guidance for integrating AI into academic integrity policies
- Classroom practice recommendations
- Professional development resources
- Assessment frameworks for evaluating AI impact
Related Resources
- George Mason University: Strategies for Teaching Well When Students Have Access to AI
- Stanford's guidelines for AI in education
- IU's data security policies for AI tools

title: A Curated Guide to AI Resources for Computing Faculty
summary: The internet is flooded with AI resources. This practical, curated guide helps computing faculty find the most useful tools, prompts, and strategies—without the noise.
content_plain:
The internet is flooded with AI resources. This practical, curated guide helps computing faculty find the most useful tools, prompts, and strategies—without the noise. As faculty in Luddy, you are in a unique position. You aren't just figuring out how to use Generative AI; you are teaching the students who will build, deploy, and critically evaluate these tools for years to come.
The challenge is twofold:
- How can you leverage AI to improve your own teaching?
- How can you effectively integrate AI into your curriculum as a core competency?
The internet is flooded with AI resources, and it's impossible to sift through them all. This post is a practical, curated guide to help you find the most useful resources for your courses without the noise.
Start with IU: Key Local Resources
Before diving into the wider web, start with the excellent resources available directly from IU:
- Generative AI 101 Faculty Resources — An overview of the GenAI 101 Course available to all at IU, plus a syllabus insert for promoting the course to students.
- Kelley School of Business "AI Playbook" — A living guide for faculty on the use of generative AI in teaching, grading, and research.
A Quick Starting Point: Three Actionable Resources
1. Teach CS with AI — A hub specifically for integrating AI into CS courses with lesson plans, project ideas, and pedagogical strategies.
2. Harvard "Teaching with Gen-AI" — High-level guidance on course design with excellent case studies and strategies for handling risks.
3. AI for Education: Effective Prompting — A focused guide on how to write better prompts, including the "5 S Framework."
The Deep Dive: Curated Resource Library
Using AI in Your Classroom
- Derek Bok Center (Harvard): "Teaching in the Age of AI" — Concrete assignment ideas and sample activities.
- One Useful Thing: "15 Times to Use AI and 5 Not To" — Easy-to-digest guidance on where AI is helpful and where it isn't.
- Syllabi Policies for AI Generative Tools — A collection of sample policies from other educators.
Helping Students Get Better at Prompting
- AI for Education: Prompt Library — Searchable, ready-to-use prompts for educators.
- More Useful Things: Prompt Repository — Tested prompt sets curated by Ethan and Lilach Mollick.
- Anthropic Prompt Library — Industry-standard examples of effective prompting.
Teaching AI in CS/INF Courses
- Teach CS with AI Resource Hub — Dedicated to integrating AI topics in CS courses.
- metaLAB at Harvard: AI Pedagogy Project — Assignments and projects focused on critical AI thinking.
- AI for Education: Critical Analysis Activities — Activities for students to evaluate AI outputs and ethics.
Building Your Own AI Tools
- HBSP: Custom AI Chatbots — Walk-through for designing classroom chatbots.
- OpenAI: Practical Guide to Building Agents — Technical guide for advanced projects.
Professional Development
- IBM Skills Build for Educators — Structured PD path for AI fluency.
- University of Maine: LearnWithAI — Practical how-to for faculty integration.
Conclusion: Start Small, Start Now
You don't need to redesign your entire curriculum overnight. Pick one thing to try this month. By experimenting now, you'll be better prepared to lead your students in this new, AI-driven landscape.

title: Teaching Workload Planner
summary: A customizable planning document to help faculty navigate their teaching load—with strategies for streamlining work and maintaining quality.
content_plain:
A customizable planning document to help faculty navigate their teaching load—with strategies for streamlining work and maintaining quality. This customizable Teaching Workload Planner was designed by Loleen Berdahl to help faculty work through a personal plan for navigating their teaching load.
Two Categories of Planning
Things Within My Personal Control
- Course preparation strategies
- Grading and feedback approaches
- Office hours and student communication
- Assignment design and revision
Opportunities to Streamline My Workload
- Syllabus design bootcamp resources
- Strategies for developing efficient assignments
- Suggestions for minimizing academic misconduct
- Leveraging teaching assistants and technology
Key Strategies
1. Prioritize ruthlessly: Not everything needs equal attention. Focus your energy where it has the most impact on student learning.
2. Batch similar tasks: Grade all of one assignment type at once rather than switching between tasks.
3. Reuse and iterate: Build on what worked last semester rather than starting from scratch.
4. Set time limits: Allocate specific time blocks for grading and preparation, then stop.
5. Seek support: Use institutional resources, collaborate with colleagues, and delegate when possible.

title: Decoding Student Learning: Finding and Fixing Bottlenecks
summary: A framework for identifying where students get stuck and developing targeted interventions—based on David Pace's Decoding the Disciplines methodology.
content_plain:
A framework for identifying where students get stuck and developing targeted interventions—based on David Pace's Decoding the Disciplines methodology. Decoding student learning is a framework for understanding where students are struggling and how to address those struggles. It's a way of asking questions about your situation and systematically exploring it to find out what is needed.
What is Decoding Student Learning?
Decoding student learning is a strategy for identifying the bottlenecks in student learning and developing targeted interventions to address those bottlenecks. Originally developed by David Pace and Joan Middendorf at Indiana University, the approach has been applied across disciplines.
The 7 Questions of Decoding
1. Where is student learning being blocked? Where are the bottlenecks?
2. What exactly do students have to do to succeed? What specific skills or knowledge do they need?
3. What are the mental moves students need to make? What cognitive processes must they use?
4. How do I model this for students? How can I show them the process?
5. How do I provide practice and feedback? How can students rehearse with guidance?
6. How do I assess student learning? How can I measure whether they're succeeding?
7. How do I adjust my instruction? How can I refine based on results?
The Interview Technique
The interview technique involves asking students to describe a task they're struggling with, then probing their thought process:
 Step Description 
 
 Identify the task Ask students to describe what they're struggling with 
 Elaborate Ask them to walk through their thinking process 
 Break it down Identify the specific skills or knowledge gaps 
 Model the process Show students how experts approach the same task 
 Provide feedback Give targeted guidance on their approach 
Why This Matters in Computing
In computing courses, students often hit bottlenecks that aren't purely technical:
- Algorithmic thinking: Students may struggle to break problems into steps before coding
- Debugging mindset: Moving from "it doesn't work" to systematic diagnosis
- Abstraction: Seeing patterns across different implementations
- Reading others' code: A skill rarely taught but constantly needed
The Connection to AI
Research shows that to effectively use AI tools, students need a strong foundation in problem decomposition and critical thinking. Students who can't break down a problem manually won't write good prompts for AI either.
Getting Started
Pick one assignment where students consistently struggle. Ask yourself: what mental move am I expecting them to make that I've never explicitly taught? That's your first bottleneck to decode.
Based on extended notes from the Decoding the Disciplines conversation with David Pace.

title: Interactive Lecturing: Beyond the Traditional Lecture
summary: Strategies for making lectures interactive—from preview slides and prior knowledge activation to embedded practice and peer discussion.
content_plain:
Strategies for making lectures interactive—from preview slides and prior knowledge activation to embedded practice and peer discussion. Interactive lecturing is defined as "the process of combining engaging presentations with carefully selected active learning methods to achieve intended learning goals" (Barkley & Major).
Traditional lectures may feel efficient, but when instructors only profess what they know without interruption, students miss opportunities to think critically and meaningfully apply information.
Strategies for Interactive Lectures
Preview of Class Slide
Provide students with a snapshot of what to expect during the session. This serves as an advance organizer that helps students structure their learning.
Activate Prior Knowledge
Start with a reflective question that allows students to think about the topic based on their own experiences. Give time to write down what they know.
Chunk and Check
Break lectures into 10-15 minute segments. Between segments, insert brief activities:
- Minute papers: Students write the most important point or a remaining question
- Turn and talk: Students discuss a question with a neighbor for 2 minutes
- Polling: Use classroom response systems for quick comprehension checks
Pause Procedure
Pause every 12-18 minutes for 2-3 minutes. Students review notes, fill in gaps, and discuss with peers. Research shows this significantly improves retention.
Bookend Activities
- Opening: Activate prior knowledge or pose a motivating question
- Closing: Summarize key points, identify remaining questions, preview next session
Implementation Tips
- You don't have to use every technique every class
- Start with one or two strategies and build from there
- Explain to students why you're using interactive techniques
- Be comfortable with productive noise and movement in the classroom

title: The Hidden Curriculum: What Students Don't Know They Don't Know
summary: The unwritten rules of academia—from when to raise your hand to how to email a professor—can derail students who lack the cultural capital to navigate them.
content_plain:
The unwritten rules of academia—from when to raise your hand to how to email a professor—can derail students who lack the cultural capital to navigate them. The hidden curriculum refers to the unwritten rules and expectations that govern behavior in academic settings. It encompasses the cultural capital, social norms, and unspoken assumptions that students are expected to understand and navigate in order to succeed.
What Is the Hidden Curriculum?
Every classroom has two curricula running simultaneously:
1. The explicit curriculum: What's on the syllabus—learning objectives, assignments, grading criteria
2. The hidden curriculum: The unspoken rules about how to behave, communicate, and succeed
For students from backgrounds where family members attended college, many of these rules feel intuitive. For first-generation students, transfer students, or students from different cultural backgrounds, these unwritten expectations can feel like a minefield.
Common Decision Points
Students constantly face hidden-curriculum decisions they may not even recognize:
 Decision Point What's Really Being Asked 
 
 Is it okay to raise my hand? How does participation work in this class? 
 Should I email the professor? What communication norms exist? 
 Is it essential to know now, or can I wait? How do I prioritize questions? 
 Should I go to office hours? Is this for struggling students or everyone? 
 Can I disagree with the professor? What does intellectual discourse look like here? 
Why This Matters in Computing
Computing courses layer additional hidden curricula on top of academic norms:
- When to ask for help vs. "figure it out" — The line between persistence and stubbornness
- How to read documentation — A skill experts take for granted
- What "done" means — Code that compiles? Passes tests? Is maintainable?
- How to collaborate on code — Git etiquette, code review norms, pair programming expectations
Strategies for Making the Hidden Visible
1. Name It Explicitly
When you catch yourself assuming students know something, stop and teach it. "You might not know this, but office hours aren't just for students who are struggling..."
2. Create Decision Flowcharts
Post visual guides for common scenarios: "Stuck on a bug? Here's a decision tree for when to Google, when to ask a peer, and when to come to office hours."
3. Share Expert Thinking Aloud
When demonstrating a problem, narrate your internal monologue: "My first instinct here is to check the error message... I'm not sure what this means, so I'd Google this exact phrase..."
4. Normalize Not Knowing
Explicitly model uncertainty and the process of finding answers. This is especially powerful in computing, where even senior developers constantly look things up.
The Advising Connection
Advisors play a critical role in decoding the hidden curriculum. They can help students navigate academic environments, make informed decisions, and develop the skills needed to succeed. As the saying goes: "Advising is teaching."
Based on the Decoding the Disciplines framework and research on cultural capital in higher education.

title: The Exam Debrief: Turning Assessments into Learning Opportunities
summary: Exam debriefs help students identify self-defeating behaviors, understand misconceptions, and develop better study strategies for future assessments.
content_plain:
Exam debriefs help students identify self-defeating behaviors, understand misconceptions, and develop better study strategies for future assessments. Exam debriefs offer faculty the opportunity to confront study strategy issues and understand misconceptions students may hold about the content. There is a window of opportunity immediately following an exam to help students identify what they did to prepare and what they could do better next time.
The Exam Debrief Process
Part 1: Analyze Missed Questions
Students look carefully at the questions they missed and try to determine why each question was missed. Was it:
- A misunderstanding of the concept?
- A careless error?
- A topic they didn't study?
- A question format they found confusing?
Part 2: Find Patterns
Students examine their missed questions to see if a pattern emerges. Did they miss questions for the same reason? Common patterns include:
- Not reading questions carefully
- Confusing similar concepts
- Running out of time
- Skipping certain types of questions
Part 3: Study Strategy Review
Students prepare a brief description of how they studied for the exam:
- Amount of time devoted to studying
- Methods used (re-reading, practice problems, flashcards, etc.)
- Whether they studied alone or with others
- When they started preparing
Part 4: Action Plan
Based on parts 1-3, students create a specific plan for the next exam:
- What study strategies will they change?
- How will they address the patterns they identified?
- What resources will they use?
Why It Works
- Builds metacognitive awareness
- Shifts focus from grades to learning
- Helps students develop transferable study skills
- Creates a growth mindset around assessment
- Provides instructors with insights into common misconceptions
Tips for Implementation
1. Schedule the debrief within one class session of returning the exam
2. Keep it structured but reflective—provide guiding questions
3. Make it low-stakes (participation, not graded for content)
4. Follow up by checking in on students' action plans before the next exam

title: Mid-Semester Feedback: Questions Beyond the Basics
summary: Go beyond 'what's working?' with targeted mid-semester feedback questions about inclusive teaching, classroom dynamics, pacing, and content mastery.
content_plain:
Go beyond 'what's working?' with targeted mid-semester feedback questions about inclusive teaching, classroom dynamics, pacing, and content mastery. Mid-semester feedback lets instructors "check the pulse" of their class partway through the semester. By surveying students—especially after a chance to evaluate evidence of learning—instructors and students alike can gain practical and actionable insights about what is working as well as recommendations for improving learning and teaching.
Beyond the standard questions, you may want to ask more specific questions about your course goals, inclusive teaching practice, classroom dynamics, pacing, and content.
General Questions (Start Here)
- What is helping your learning in this class?
- What is hindering your learning in this class?
- What could the instructor change to improve your learning experience?
- What could you do differently to improve your own learning?
Inclusive Teaching Questions
- Does the instructor make you feel that you belong in the class? What suggestion do you have for promoting a greater sense of belonging?
- How comfortable do you feel participating in the class? Is there anything the instructor could do to encourage more equitable participation?
- What does the instructor do to foster mutual respect among students? What else could they do?
Classroom Dynamics
- How well do group activities contribute to your learning? What improvements would you suggest?
- Do you feel comfortable asking questions during class? If not, what barriers exist?
- How effectively does the instructor manage class time?
Pacing and Content
- Is the pace of the course too fast, too slow, or about right?
- Which topics or concepts have been most challenging? What would help?
- Are the assignments aligned with what's being taught in class?
- Do you feel prepared for assessments based on what's covered in class?
Technology and Resources
- Are the course materials (slides, videos, readings) helpful for your learning?
- Is the Canvas course site easy to navigate?
- Are there additional resources that would support your learning?
Tips for Effective Mid-Semester Feedback
1. Keep it short — 5-7 questions maximum. Students won't complete a 20-question survey.
2. Make it anonymous — Students are more honest when they can't be identified.
3. Close the loop — Share a summary of what you heard and what you'll change. This is the most important step.
4. Act on something — Even one small change shows students their feedback matters.
5. Don't take it personally — Focus on actionable items, not emotional reactions.
When to Survey
The ideal window is weeks 5-7—early enough to make meaningful changes, late enough that students have experienced your teaching approach.
Questions adapted from the Poorvu Center for Teaching and Learning and the University Center for Teaching and Learning.

title: Universal Design for Learning 3.0: What's New and Why It Matters
summary: UDL 3.0 expands beyond the original framework with new guidelines for welcoming interests and identities, nurturing joy, and fostering belonging in higher education.
content_plain:
UDL 3.0 expands beyond the original framework with new guidelines for welcoming interests and identities, nurturing joy, and fostering belonging in higher education. Universal Design for Learning (UDL) has been a cornerstone framework for inclusive education. With the release of UDL 3.0, the guidelines have expanded to address dimensions of learning that go beyond traditional access and engagement.
What Is UDL?
UDL is a framework for designing flexible learning experiences that accommodate individual differences. Rather than retrofitting accommodations after the fact, UDL builds flexibility into the design from the start.
The core principle: design for the margins, and you improve the experience for everyone.
What's New in UDL 3.0?
The updated guidelines expand the original framework with a stronger emphasis on:
Welcoming Interests and Identities (Guideline 7)
- 7.1: Value learners' assets and lived experiences
- 7.2: Cultivate interests and intrinsic motivation
- 7.3: Nurture joy and play
Fostering Belonging and Community (Guideline 8)
- 8.1: Foster a climate of trust and mutual respect
- 8.2: Promote collaborative learning communities
- 8.3: Develop learner agency
Sustaining Effort and Persistence (Guideline 9)
- 9.1: Promote expectations that optimize challenge
- 9.2: Foster strategies and supports for coping
- 9.3: Develop habits of mind for lifelong learning
Why This Matters for Computing Faculty
Computing courses present unique UDL opportunities and challenges:
- Multiple means of representation: Code examples, diagrams, pseudocode, verbal walkthroughs, video demonstrations
- Multiple means of engagement: Individual coding, pair programming, group projects, competitions, open-ended exploration
- Multiple means of action and expression: Written reports, code submissions, oral presentations, video demos, portfolio projects
Practical Quick Wins
This Week
- Add captions to any video content you create
- Provide lecture slides before class, not after
- Offer at least two ways to demonstrate mastery on your next assignment
This Month
- Review your syllabus for welcoming language
- Create a "Getting Started" guide that makes hidden expectations explicit
- Ask students how they prefer to receive feedback
This Semester
- Build choice into at least one major assignment
- Implement a flexible deadline policy for low-stakes work
- Create alternative pathways for students who need them
The 9-in-9 Overtime Series
The "9-in-9 Overtime" series offers free webinars delivering focused, actionable UDL 3.0 strategies specifically designed for higher education. Each 60-minute session provides practical tools and resources readily adaptable to any discipline.
For more information, visit the UDL Guidelines.

title: A No-Documentation Missed Exam Policy That Actually Works
summary: A copy-paste-ready exam make-up policy that protects student privacy, avoids overwhelming support offices, reduces policing, and stays equitable for everyone.
content_plain:
A copy-paste-ready exam make-up policy that protects student privacy, avoids overwhelming support offices, reduces policing, and stays equitable for everyone. Balancing compassion with boundaries when students miss exams is one of the most common challenges faculty face—especially in large-enrollment courses. Here's a practical, copy-paste-ready policy framework.
Core Principles
- We prioritize learning, privacy, and fairness
- Short-term illness does not require medical documentation
- Make-ups are structured, time-bound, and consistent
The Token System
Eligibility & Notification
- Short-term illness or emergency: Email as soon as possible (ideally before the exam). No medical details needed.
- One no-questions-asked token: Each student has one "illness/emergency token" per term for a major assessment.
- Non-emergency conflicts: Not covered; request in advance per syllabus guidelines.
What's Not Covered
Ongoing or repeated absences (2+ major assessments) signal a broader need. Students should meet with their academic advisor to discuss options, including withdrawal timelines.
Timelines & Process
- Request window: Student must email within 24 hours of the missed exam
- Make-up window: Complete within 5 business days of the original exam
- Format: May be a different but equivalent version; academic integrity rules fully apply
- Limit: Only one major assessment may be made up via the token
Low-Stakes Work Policy
To reduce stress and policing for short absences:
- Drop policy: Drop the lowest 2 quizzes (or X% of low-stakes items)
- No retroactive make-ups for low-stakes items beyond that drop
- Optional alternative: One "quiz bundle" reflection to recover up to 50% of points for up to 2 missed quizzes
Communication Templates
If a student reports illness before the exam:
"Thanks for letting me know. Please focus on your health. You may use your no-questions-asked make-up token for this exam. Reply within 24 hours to confirm. The make-up will occur by [date]; details to follow. No medical documentation is required."
If absences are recurring:
"I'm noticing multiple assessments affected. At this point, the best next step is to meet with your academic advisor to review options and deadlines. After that conversation, email me so we can determine what's feasible going forward."
Large-Class Logistics
- Use a Canvas "Self-Attestation Quiz" (1–2 questions: "I'm using my token," "I understand the 5-day window")
- Publish one common make-up slot rather than case-by-case meetings
- Create an alternate exam bank in advance
- Post a one-page flowchart in Canvas
Why This Is Fair
- Students who show up aren't disadvantaged: limits, timelines, and equivalent rigor keep grading credible
- Students with legitimate issues get a humane on-ramp without paperwork barriers
- You avoid overload: no doctor's notes, minimal negotiations, fewer referrals
- Clarity reduces conflict: expectations are public and consistent
Adjustable Settings
 Setting Option A Option B 
 
 Make-up window 3 business days 5 business days 
 "Forgot" consequence Zero One-time 50% capped alternative 
 Low-stakes drops 2 drops 1 drop + 1 reflection recovery

title: AI Teaching and Learning Podcasts for Faculty
summary: Curated podcast recommendations for faculty who want to stay current on AI in education—from redesigning courses with AI to academic integrity in the age of generative tools.
content_plain:
Curated podcast recommendations for faculty who want to stay current on AI in education—from redesigning courses with AI to academic integrity in the age of generative tools. Staying current on AI in education doesn't always mean reading another report. Podcasts offer a low-effort way to absorb new ideas during your commute, workout, or grading breaks.
Recommended Podcasts
What If College Teaching Was Redesigned With AI In Mind?
The Learning Curve Podcast
A former university president tries to reimagine college teaching with AI in mind. Features Paul LeBlanc discussing his vision and Maha Bali providing reaction from a teaching expert's perspective.
Listen on Learning Curve FM
The Opposite of Cheating
Spotify
Based on the book by Tricia Bertram Gallant and David Rettinger, this podcast explores teaching for integrity in the age of AI. Episodes cover practical strategies for designing assessments that promote authentic learning.
Listen on Spotify
Recommended Newsletters
For those who prefer reading:
- The Rundown — Daily newsletter summarizing AI news across research, policy, and industry
- The Neuron — Broad coverage of emerging AI trends with education-adjacent insights
- The Batch — Weekly deep dives into AI research, tools, and development from DeepLearning.AI
- The Algorithmic Bridge — Thoughtful essays analyzing AI's social, ethical, and educational impact
- Everyday AI Newsletter — Daily newsletter and podcast aimed at making AI accessible to non-specialists
How to Use These Resources
1. Pick one podcast and one newsletter — Don't try to follow everything
2. Listen with a purpose — Before each episode, ask: "What one idea could I try in my course?"
3. Share with colleagues — Forward interesting episodes to your teaching community
4. Keep a running list — Note ideas that resonate; review them before planning next semester
The goal isn't to become an AI expert. It's to stay informed enough to make thoughtful decisions about your teaching.

title: Using VoiceThread in Canvas for Interactive Discussions
summary: VoiceThread enables asynchronous multimedia discussions where students respond with voice, video, or text—creating richer engagement than traditional discussion boards.
content_plain:
VoiceThread enables asynchronous multimedia discussions where students respond with voice, video, or text—creating richer engagement than traditional discussion boards. Traditional discussion boards often produce formulaic responses: a required post plus two obligatory replies. VoiceThread offers a richer alternative that supports multiple modes of communication.
What Is VoiceThread?
VoiceThread is a collaborative multimedia tool that allows instructors and students to create interactive presentations and discussions. Unlike traditional discussion boards, participants can respond using:
- Voice (audio recording)
- Video (webcam recording)
- Text (typed comments)
- Doodles (drawing on slides)
Why Use VoiceThread?
Richer Student Engagement
When students hear each other's voices or see each other's faces, discussions feel more personal. This is especially valuable in online and hybrid courses where building community is challenging.
Accessibility
Multiple response modes accommodate different learning preferences and abilities. A student who struggles with writing can record a video; a student uncomfortable on camera can use audio or text.
Asynchronous Flexibility
Students can participate on their own schedule while still experiencing the "flow" of a conversation—each response builds on previous ones.
Practical Uses in Computing Courses
- Code Walkthroughs: Record yourself explaining a solution, then ask students to respond with their approach
- Design Critiques: Share a UI mockup or system diagram; students annotate with doodles and audio
- Debugging Exercises: Post buggy code; students record their debugging process as they find and explain issues
- Peer Teaching: Students create short VoiceThread lessons on concepts they've mastered
Getting Started
1. VoiceThread integrates directly with Canvas as an LTI tool
2. Create a VoiceThread by uploading slides, images, or documents
3. Add it to your Canvas module as a lesson, discussion, or assignment
4. Set response requirements (e.g., "respond with audio to at least 2 peers")
Tips for Success
- Model the format first — Create a sample VoiceThread and walk students through responding
- Start small — Use it for one low-stakes discussion before making it a graded assignment
- Set clear expectations — Specify response length (e.g., "60-90 seconds of audio")
- Grade for substance, not production quality — Students shouldn't feel pressure to produce polished videos

title: Trauma-Informed Pedagogy: Teaching With Care in Challenging Times
summary: Students carry invisible burdens into every classroom. Trauma-informed teaching creates conditions where learning is possible without requiring students to disclose their struggles.
content_plain:
Students carry invisible burdens into every classroom. Trauma-informed teaching creates conditions where learning is possible without requiring students to disclose their struggles. Students carry invisible burdens into every classroom. Financial stress, family responsibilities, health challenges, discrimination, and the general weight of navigating young adulthood in uncertain times all affect how students show up—or don't—for learning.
Trauma-informed pedagogy doesn't require you to be a therapist. It requires you to design learning environments where students can engage even when they're not at their best.
Core Principles
1. Safety
Students need to feel physically and psychologically safe to take intellectual risks. In computing courses, this means:
- Normalizing errors and debugging as learning, not failure
- Creating space for questions without judgment
- Being predictable in your expectations and responses
2. Trustworthiness and Transparency
- Follow through on what you say you'll do
- Explain the reasoning behind your policies
- Be honest about what you don't know
3. Choice and Collaboration
- Offer multiple pathways to demonstrate learning when possible
- Let students have agency in how they engage (cameras on/off, solo vs. group work)
- Involve students in creating class norms
4. Empowerment
- Focus on what students can do, not what they can't
- Recognize effort and progress, not just outcomes
- Help students develop self-advocacy skills
Practical Strategies
Syllabus Design
- Include a "Support Resources" section prominently (not buried on page 12)
- Write in a warm, inviting tone—the syllabus is your first impression
- State your flexibility policies clearly so students don't have to ask
Daily Teaching
- Start class with a brief connection moment (a question, a check-in, a relevant news item)
- Avoid cold-calling unless students have opted in
- Provide content warnings when course material touches on sensitive topics
Assessment
- Build in flexibility: drop lowest scores, allow revisions, offer alternatives
- Separate learning from compliance—don't penalize students for process issues
- Give feedback that focuses on growth, not deficiency
Communication
- Respond to student emails with warmth, even when setting boundaries
- When a student disengages, reach out with curiosity rather than judgment
- "I noticed you've missed a few classes—everything okay?" goes further than "You've exceeded your allowed absences"
What This Is Not
Trauma-informed teaching is not:
- Lowering standards or expectations
- Removing accountability
- Being a therapist or counselor
- Ignoring deadlines or requirements
It is about creating conditions where the maximum number of students can actually learn.
Know Your Resources
Know how to refer students to campus support services:
- Counseling and Psychological Services
- Student Care and Resource Center
- Accessible Educational Services
- Food pantry and emergency financial assistance
You don't need to solve students' problems. You need to know where to point them.

title: Peer Instruction in Computing: Beyond Think-Pair-Share
summary: Peer instruction—where students discuss conceptual questions with classmates—consistently improves learning outcomes in CS. Here's how to implement it effectively.
content_plain:
Peer instruction—where students discuss conceptual questions with classmates—consistently improves learning outcomes in CS. Here's how to implement it effectively. Peer instruction, pioneered by Eric Mazur in physics and adapted for computing by Beth Simon and others, is one of the most studied and effective active learning strategies available.
The core idea is simple: pose a conceptual question, have students think individually, discuss with a neighbor, and revote. The magic happens in the discussion—students who just figured something out are often better at explaining it than experts who figured it out decades ago.
How Peer Instruction Works
1. Pose a question — Display a multiple-choice conceptual question
2. Individual vote — Students answer independently (via clicker, Top Hat, or hand signals)
3. Check results — If 30-70% got it right, proceed to discussion. If >70%, move on. If <30%, reteach.
4. Peer discussion — Students turn to a neighbor and explain their reasoning (2-3 minutes)
5. Revote — Students answer again after discussion
6. Instructor explanation — Brief wrap-up addressing common misconceptions
Why It Works in Computing
Conceptual Understanding Gaps
Many CS students can write code that works without understanding why it works. Peer instruction surfaces these gaps.
The Curse of Knowledge
Faculty who have understood pointers for 20 years struggle to remember why they're confusing. A student who figured it out 5 minutes ago doesn't have this problem.
Active Processing
Explaining your reasoning to someone else is one of the most effective learning strategies known. It forces you to organize your thinking and identify gaps.
Writing Good Peer Instruction Questions
Characteristics of Effective Questions
- Target a single concept or common misconception
- Have plausible wrong answers (distractors based on real student errors)
- Cannot be solved by pattern matching or formula application
- Require reasoning, not just recall
Example: Recursion
