title: Empower Your Work with AI: A Beginner's Guide to ChatGPT for Faculty
summary: A practical, jargon-free introduction to ChatGPT for faculty—covering what it can do, what it can't, and how to start using it for teaching tasks today.
content_plain:
A practical, jargon-free introduction to ChatGPT for faculty—covering what it can do, what it can't, and how to start using it for teaching tasks today. If you've been hearing about ChatGPT but haven't tried it yet—or tried it once and weren't sure what to do with it—this guide is for you.
What Is ChatGPT?
ChatGPT is a large language model that generates text based on patterns it learned from training data. Think of it as a very sophisticated autocomplete that can:
- Write drafts (emails, rubrics, assignment descriptions)
- Explain concepts at different levels
- Generate examples and practice problems
- Summarize long texts
- Help brainstorm ideas
What It Cannot Do
- Think: It predicts likely next words, not truth
- Fact-check: It can generate plausible-sounding but incorrect information
- Understand context: It doesn't know your students, your course, or your institution
- Replace judgment: It's a tool, not a colleague
Five Ways to Start This Week
1. Draft a Rubric
"Create a rubric for a 2-page reflection paper on database design ethics. Include 4 criteria with descriptors for Excellent, Satisfactory, and Needs Improvement."
2. Generate Practice Problems
"Write 5 multiple-choice questions about binary search trees. Include one common misconception as a distractor for each question. Provide the correct answers and brief explanations."
3. Simplify Your Syllabus Language
"Rewrite this syllabus paragraph in plain language at a 10th-grade reading level, keeping the policies unchanged: [paste paragraph]"
4. Create a Code Explanation
"Explain this Python function line by line as if you were talking to a student who understands loops but hasn't seen recursion yet: [paste code]"
5. Brainstorm Discussion Prompts
"Generate 5 discussion questions about the ethical implications of facial recognition technology for an introductory informatics course."
Tips for Better Results
- Be specific — The more context you provide, the better the output
- Iterate — Your first prompt rarely produces the best result. Refine and ask again.
- Verify everything — Always review AI output before using it with students
- State the audience — "For undergraduate CS students" produces different results than a generic request
- Ask for alternatives — "Give me three different approaches to..." leverages AI's breadth
Setting Boundaries
When using AI in your teaching workflow:
- Do use AI for: First drafts, brainstorming, formatting, generating examples
- Don't use AI for: Final assessment decisions, student evaluations, sensitive communications
- Always: Review and edit AI output before sharing it
- Consider: Being transparent with students about how you use AI in course preparation
Your First Assignment
Pick one task from your to-do list this week—a rubric, a set of practice problems, an email template. Try using ChatGPT to create a first draft. Then edit it. You'll quickly develop a sense for where AI saves time and where it falls short.

title: Cut and Paste AI Prompts for Teaching
summary: A comprehensive guide of ready-to-use AI prompts for course design, assessment development, and lesson planning—designed to help faculty experiment with AI tools more confidently.
content_plain:
A comprehensive guide of ready-to-use AI prompts for course design, assessment development, and lesson planning—designed to help faculty experiment with AI tools more confidently. Dr. Cynthia Alby developed the resource "Cut and Paste AI Prompts" for instructors. This comprehensive guide intends to provide instructors not only with prompts, but techniques to effectively experiment with various AI tools specific to various practices of teaching.
As Dr. Alby notes: "I have found that providing instructors with prompts they can cut and paste into AI made them more comfortable, more quickly with experimenting. I also discovered that when I can help someone experiment with AI for an hour or so, their anxiety levels tend to drop, and they begin to see it less like a threat and more like a tool."
Course Design Prompts
Aligning Learning Objectives:
"Review these learning objectives for my [course name] course. Suggest ways to strengthen alignment with Bloom's Taxonomy and identify any gaps in cognitive levels covered."
Creating Module Overviews:
"Create a 3-week module plan for teaching [topic] to undergraduate students in [discipline]. Include learning objectives, key concepts, activities, and assessments."
Assessment Development Prompts
Rubric Generation:
"Create a detailed rubric for a [assignment type] in [course]. Include 4 criteria with descriptions for Excellent, Proficient, Developing, and Beginning performance levels."
Question Writing:
"Generate 10 multiple-choice questions about [topic] for an introductory [discipline] course. Include plausible distractors based on common misconceptions."
Lesson Planning Prompts
Active Learning Activities:
"Suggest 3 active learning activities I could use to teach [concept] in a 75-minute class session. Include setup instructions and estimated time for each."
Discussion Prompts:
"Generate 5 thought-provoking discussion questions about [topic] that would work well for small group discussions in an undergraduate [discipline] course."
Tips for Better Results
1. Be specific about context — Include course level, student population, and discipline
2. Iterate on outputs — Use follow-up prompts to refine initial results
3. Verify accuracy — Always review AI-generated content before using
4. Adapt to your voice — Edit outputs to match your teaching style
Getting Started
Start with one prompt this week. Try generating a rubric or a set of discussion questions. Edit the output to fit your needs. The goal is to reduce the friction of starting from scratch, not to use AI output verbatim.

title: Seven Ways to Use Video in Your Course
summary: From pre-lecture content to expert interviews, practical strategies for integrating video into your teaching—regardless of modality—with evidence-based best practices.
content_plain:
From pre-lecture content to expert interviews, practical strategies for integrating video into your teaching—regardless of modality—with evidence-based best practices. There are several ways that you can use videos in your course, regardless of modality. Here are seven evidence-based strategies:
1. Introduce a Class or Unit
Create pre-lecture videos for students to watch prior to attending class. This provides students with an initial exposure to the content, sparks interest, and improves students' understanding. The visuals in your video can help them connect with the material.
2. Build Background Knowledge
We know that students learn best when they take in information via multiple modalities—through reading, listening to the instructor's oral explanations, hands-on activities and viewing visual media. Images and videos support the learning of new content, concepts, and ideas.
3. Emphasize a Point with Microlectures
Identify key learning goals or areas where students have difficulty understanding and create a short microlecture to support students. These can be used for flipped classroom application or for independent student review.
Key tip: Keep these videos focused by only discussing the learning goal. Avoid adding fun facts or information not directly related to the topic.
4. Demonstrate Examples
Instead of providing students with an answer key to a problem, create a video (with narration) working out the solution step-by-step. To encourage students to watch the video:
- Work out only part of the problem in the video
- Have students complete the rest and submit it as a low-stakes assignment
- Use PlayPosit to integrate questions into your videos for immediate feedback
5. Interview an Expert
Record interviews with experts in the discipline, providing examples and explaining concepts relevant to what is being covered in class. This enriches students' learning by allowing them to hear what other experts have to say about a particular topic.
6. Dispel Misconceptions
A video that explicitly dispels common misconceptions about a topic can help students achieve conceptual change. This can be as simple as a video that starts with "you might think that… but you'd be wrong."
Simply presenting facts can reinforce students' incorrect assumptions. Deliberately using a video that presents incorrect information then discussing these mistakes can also be effective.
7. Make Video Part of Larger Assignments
Research shows that videos embedded in larger homework assignments can be highly effective. Students valued the videos and showed improved understanding of difficult concepts compared to semesters when videos weren't used.
Best Practices
- Keep it short: 6-9 minutes maximum before attention drops off
- Add interactivity: Use embedded questions with tools like PlayPosit
- Caption everything: Required for accessibility and helpful for all learners
- Be yourself: Students respond to authenticity, not production quality

title: Building Relationships with Students: Practical Strategies
summary: Research shows strong correlations between faculty-student rapport and academic achievement. Here are practical strategies for learning names and building connections.
content_plain:
Research shows strong correlations between faculty-student rapport and academic achievement. Here are practical strategies for learning names and building connections. Researchers have found strong positive correlations between faculty building relationships and rapport with students and academic achievement, attendance, student interest, motivation, empowerment, self-efficacy, student attention, classroom behaviors, and interactions.
As the Eberly Center at Carnegie Mellon notes: "Even in large lecture classes, it is possible to get to know a conspicuous number of your students, a few at a time. For students, it's the effort that counts."
Learning Student Names
Use Technology to Help
- Name Coach: Students record their names with phonetic pronunciation. The tool slows down recordings to help you hear pronunciation correctly.
- Canvas Photo Roster: Available in the People menu, displays names, photos, and NameCoach recordings for each student.
- Print attendance sheets with space for notes about each student.
In-Class Strategies
- Name Tents: Have students write their names on folded index cards for the first few weeks
- Seating Charts: Ask students to stay in the same seats for at least two weeks while you learn names
- Have students say their names when asking or answering questions
Introduce Yourself
Consider sharing information beyond your name and the course title:
- Personal biography: Hobbies, interests, how you came to the field
- Educational biography: Your research, current projects, future plans
- Teaching biography: What you enjoy about teaching, what you learn from students
Build Connection Through Structure
One-on-One or Small Group Meetings
"Meaningful interactions with students outside of classes is listed by the National Survey of Student Engagement as a high-impact educational practice."
Set up brief meet-and-greet appointments in the first weeks. Your primary goal: get to know students as people. "I care about you first and foremost as a person—I want you to be successful in this class."
Beyond Office Hours
- Arrive to class a little early and stay a little later to chat
- This allows students who aren't comfortable raising questions during class to approach you in a low-pressure way
Icebreakers
Use small icebreakers beyond the first day to continue building rapport. Course-specific icebreakers can connect relationship-building to learning goals.
Student Profiles
Have students complete a brief confidential profile with:
- Background and interests
- Prior knowledge of the subject
- Expectations for the course
- Work/family constraints that might affect their schedule
Communicate Your Investment
Let students know during Week 1 that you'll use the Student Engagement Roster (SER) to provide feedback and support their success. This signals that you're paying attention and invested in their learning.

title: The 6 Rs of Teaching Reflection
summary: A framework for critical reflection on your teaching practice at the end of the semester—from reacting to data to reassessing new strategies.
content_plain:
A framework for critical reflection on your teaching practice at the end of the semester—from reacting to data to reassessing new strategies. The end of the semester is a great opportunity to reflect on teaching. Elizabeth Barkley and Claire Howell Major, in Engaged Teaching: A Handbook for College Faculty, put forth a framework for critical reflection:
The 6 Rs to Enhanced Reflection
1. Reacting
How will I decide what area of my practice to focus on?
Will this be decided by looking at data, each learner's performance, or an aspect of the curriculum?
2. Recording
How will I log my reflections?
- What documents will you use? A journal, notebook, or form?
- When will you log? Immediately after class, during, or before?
- How often will you record reflections?
How will I assess my performance?
- Will this take the form of observation, discussion, or shared planning?
- Will this be recorded by yourself, a peer, or a student?
3. Reviewing
What worked well and how do I know this?
Consider what students really understood and enjoyed about the course. How do you know improvements have been made?
What did not work as planned?
Consider what students did not engage with or found challenging, and why.
4. Revising
What will I change or adapt?
This could be a whole task or something specific. Ideas include:
- Changing from independent to paired work
- Adding scaffolding to challenging tasks
- Providing step-by-step instructions
- Making activities time-based
5. Reworking
How will I put this into place?
Consider what you'll need to do before and during class to make changes happen. What will students do differently?
What materials do I need?
What things will you need to put your revised ideas into practice?
6. Reassessing
How successful were the new strategies?
Once you've redelivered the lesson, consider: How engaged were students? How well did they understand?
What changed? Consider changes in delivery, planning, and assessment.
Freewriting Practice
Barkley suggests engaging in 5-10 minutes of freewriting that doesn't focus on a specific class session, but rather a teaching experience or moment. Write in full sentences without self-censoring or editing. This process often inspires ideas and beliefs for further exploration.
Questions to Ask Students
Consider adding reflection questions to your final exam:
- Did you give your best effort this semester? Why or why not?
- Were the strategies you used effective for this course?
- What are some things you did really well this semester?
- If you could do this course over, what would you do differently?
- What class activities or assignments helped you learn the most?

title: Creating a Grade Review Policy That Works
summary: A structured process for handling student grade disputes that requires evidence-based rationale and reduces emotional negotiations.
content_plain:
A structured process for handling student grade disputes that requires evidence-based rationale and reduces emotional negotiations. At the end of the semester, you may receive requests from students to reevaluate their final grades. If you don't already have a process outlined in your syllabus, this example from Ted M. Coopman at San José State University provides a great model.
The Key Principle
Require students to reflect on their request as they develop an evidence-based rationale for the grade change. This shifts the conversation from emotional appeal to documented performance.
Sample Policy Language
Grade Review Process
If you believe your final grade does not accurately reflect your performance, you may submit a formal grade review request. This process ensures fair consideration while requiring you to engage thoughtfully with your performance.
To request a review:
1. Wait 48 hours after grades are posted before submitting a request
2. Review the syllabus, rubrics, and assignment feedback carefully
3. Submit a written request (email or form) that includes:
 - The specific grade you are contesting
 - Your calculation of what the grade should be
 - Specific evidence supporting your case (assignment scores, rubric criteria, etc.)
 - A clear explanation of where you believe an error occurred
What is NOT grounds for review:
- Needing a higher grade for scholarships, graduate school, etc.
- Comparing your grade to other students
- Personal circumstances not previously discussed
- General dissatisfaction without specific evidence
Timeline:
- Requests must be submitted within 10 business days of grade posting
- You will receive a response within 5 business days of submission
Why This Works
- Reduces emotional appeals: Students must engage with evidence, not feelings
- Saves your time: Many students won't submit after doing the calculation
- Creates documentation: If a dispute escalates, you have a paper trail
- Promotes learning: Students engage with feedback they might otherwise ignore
Implementation Tips
1. Include this policy in your syllabus and review it before finals
2. Create a simple form or template for students to use
3. Stick to the process consistently
4. Respond professionally even to weak requests

title: Using Backchannels to Enhance Classroom Discussion
summary: A backchannel is a secondary conversation during class that can increase participation, surface questions in real-time, and support diverse learning needs.
content_plain:
A backchannel is a secondary conversation during class that can increase participation, surface questions in real-time, and support diverse learning needs. A backchannel is a conversation that takes place during an activity or event. In classroom contexts, it refers to conversations among students during a lecture or presentation. While backchannels can be disruptive (students texting about last night's game), they can also be incredibly helpful for learning.
Benefits of Intentional Backchannels
- Diversity of voices: Students who don't speak up in class can contribute
- Real-time questions: Address confusion as it arises, not after class
- Processing time: Students who need time to think can still participate
- Collaborative notes: Source class notes and resources as a group
- Question voting: See which questions are shared by multiple students
Setting Up a Backchannel in Top Hat
1. Create a new Discussion question
2. Title it "Classroom Back Channel"
3. Add instructions: "Enter a question you have that you want addressed in class. All posts are anonymous."
4. Under Response Options, set responses to be seen by "Everyone"
5. Set participant anonymity to "Participants only" (you can track comments, students cannot)
Running the Backchannel
1. Direct students to use laptops or phones to access Top Hat
2. Assign a student monitor to watch for questions
3. Open the back channel at the start of class
4. Check in with your monitor periodically
5. Address questions as they arise or batch them for designated Q&A time
Alternative Tools
- Canvas Discussions: Built-in, no additional tool needed
- Google Chat: Real-time, familiar to many students
- Microsoft Teams: Can run from a secondary device
- Slack, Discord, GroupMe: Students may already use these (note: not IU-supported)
Collaborative Note-Taking
A related strategy is collaborative note-taking, where rotating pairs of students take shared notes each class. Benefits:
- Builds accessibility into your course design
- Provides alternative to recording class sessions
- Creates a shared resource for all students
- Enables commenting and questions in the notes themselves
Use Google Docs or OneDrive for live editing and commenting.
Best Practices
- Explain the purpose: Students should understand this is for learning, not distraction
- Monitor for appropriateness: Anonymous doesn't mean unaccountable—you can track posts
- Integrate with teaching: Actually address backchannel questions
- Consider accessibility: Ensure your chosen tool works for all students

title: Key Trends from the EDUCAUSE 2023 Horizon Report
summary: The Horizon Report identifies AI-enabled learning, HyFlex modalities, and microcredentialing as key technologies and practices shaping postsecondary education.
content_plain:
The Horizon Report identifies AI-enabled learning, HyFlex modalities, and microcredentialing as key technologies and practices shaping postsecondary education. EDUCAUSE releases annual Horizon Reports identifying trends and key technologies shaping postsecondary teaching and learning. The 2023 report highlights several critical areas:
Key Technologies and Practices
1. AI-Enabled Applications for Personalized Learning
Predictive analytics and AI tools are increasingly used to:
- Identify at-risk students early
- Personalize learning pathways
- Provide adaptive feedback and practice
2. Generative AI
The report was released as generative AI tools like ChatGPT were transforming discussions about:
- Assessment design
- Academic integrity
- New skills students need
- Faculty workload and support
3. HyFlex Modalities
EDUCAUSE defines HyFlex as a course mode that gives students flexibility to choose between on-site and online instruction within the same course. Key characteristics:
- Students can move between modalities throughout the course as needed
- Same learning objectives regardless of attendance mode
- Requires intentional design for equivalent experiences
4. Microcredentialing
Microcredentials address:
- Rising cost of higher education
- Demand for flexible learning
- Employer need for demonstrated evidence of specific skills
Creating Belonging and Connectedness
A significant section of the report analyzes efforts to create a sense of belonging in students. Strategies include:
- Relationship-rich education: Intentional faculty-student and peer connections
- Inclusive pedagogy: Design that welcomes diverse learners
- Community building: Both in-person and online strategies
Implications for Faculty
Short-term
- Develop clear AI policies for your courses
- Consider how flexibility can be built into your teaching
- Focus on skills that AI cannot replace
Long-term
- Stay informed about how AI tools are evolving
- Connect your courses to career and skill outcomes
- Build connection and belonging into your course design
Accessing the Full Report
IU is an institutional member of EDUCAUSE, so content not publicly accessible should be available via SSO. The full report includes exemplar projects from colleges and universities demonstrating impact.

title: Writing Effective Course Evaluation Questions
summary: Best practices for personalizing course questionnaires—from focusing on single issues to avoiding leading questions and prioritizing actionable feedback.
content_plain:
Best practices for personalizing course questionnaires—from focusing on single issues to avoiding leading questions and prioritizing actionable feedback. The University of Michigan's Center for Research on Teaching and Learning offers the following best practices for writing customized course evaluation questions:
Best Practice 1: Focus on One Thing Only
Bad question: "How useful were the instructional materials and activities for this course?"
This asks about two things (materials AND activities). You won't know which one students are responding to.
Better: Ask separate questions about materials and activities.
Best Practice 2: Avoid Leading Questions
Bad question: "Did you learn a great amount from this course?"
This is a leading yes/no question that suggests its own answer.
Better: "To what extent do you feel you mastered the content in this course?"
This allows students to use a scale to indicate their learning rather than simply agreeing or disagreeing.
Best Practice 3: Focus on Relevant and Actionable Concepts
Students may receive up to 150 questions across their courses in a given semester. Making sure questions are relevant will increase students' likelihood of answering thoughtfully.
Ask yourself:
- Can I actually change this based on feedback?
- Is this specific to my course or generic?
- Will answers help me improve teaching?
Alternative: Exam Reflection Questions
Consider adding reflection questions at the end of your final exam:
- Did you give your best effort this semester? Why or why not?
- Were the strategies, skills, and procedures you used effective for this course?
- What are some things you did really well this semester?
- If you could do this course over, what would you do differently?
- What class activities or assignments helped you learn the most?
- What did you learn this semester, and is it important?
- What do you think you'll remember most about this class in 10 years?
Sample Custom Questions by Category
Engagement
- "How does your instructor engage your attention? Are there other approaches that would make you feel more engaged?"
Pace
- "How is the pacing of this course for you? Do you have any suggestions for improvement?"
Materials
- "Which course materials have been most useful to your learning? Least useful?"
Belonging
- "Does the instructor make you feel that you belong in the class? What suggestions do you have for promoting a greater sense of belonging?"

title: Open Educational Resources: Reducing Costs and Expanding Access
summary: Course materials can be a significant barrier for economically disadvantaged students. OER and library resources offer alternatives that reduce costs while maintaining quality.
content_plain:
Course materials can be a significant barrier for economically disadvantaged students. OER and library resources offer alternatives that reduce costs while maintaining quality. Course materials prove to be one of the areas that students, especially the economically disadvantaged, struggle with the most. The cost of required textbooks and technology can be a huge burden and hindrance for students when learning.
What Are Open Educational Resources?
Open Educational Resources (OER) are teaching, learning, and research materials that are freely available for anyone to use, adapt, and redistribute. They include:
- Textbooks and course materials
- Videos and multimedia
- Assessment tools
- Simulations and software
- Full courses
Options for Reducing Course Material Costs
1. Find Relevant Content Free Online
Check OER repositories for materials that match your learning objectives:
- OpenStax
- MIT OpenCourseWare
- OER Commons
- MERLOT
2. Use Library Resources
Most institutions have subject librarians who can help. For books needed for assignments, the library can often:
- Acquire physical or digital copies
- Make portions available through course reserves
- Help navigate fair use and copyright
3. Make Content Accessible Through Your LMS
Ensure materials are available regardless of geographical and time constraints by hosting them in Canvas.
4. Understand Fair Use
If only a small part of a book or resource is needed, the library can often make a digital copy available to the course legally.
Diversifying Course Content
Beyond cost, expanding course materials to reflect diversity prepares students for our global community. Suggestions:
Bring Other Voices Into the Room
- TED Talks
- Documentaries and podcasts
- News sources and newspapers
- Guest speakers from diverse backgrounds
Present Multiple Interaction Options
- Creating academic posters
- Digital storytelling
- Online forums and discussions
- Podcasts
Vary Examples and Contexts
- Use a variety of names, settings, and cultural references in example scenarios
- Place learning assignments in a variety of social, professional, or cultural contexts
Creating Accessible Content
Course materials need to be curated to address learner preferences:
- Caption all videos — Supports international students and varied learning paces
- Ensure screen reader compatibility — Test documents and web content
- Vary material types — Documents, audio, and video cater to different preferences
- Optimize images — Add descriptions and adjust for quality and size
Fellowship Opportunities
Many institutions offer fellowships and support for faculty transitioning to OER, such as:
- Course Material Fellowship Programs
- OER Summer Sprints
- One-on-one assistance for OER adoption

title: Working with Teaching Assistants: A Faculty Checklist
summary: A comprehensive checklist of questions to discuss with your TAs at the start of the semester—covering roles, communication, grading, and professional development.
content_plain:
A comprehensive checklist of questions to discuss with your TAs at the start of the semester—covering roles, communication, grading, and professional development. Dr. Angela Jenks and Katie Cox at UC Irvine developed a checklist for faculty who work with teaching assistants. The checklist contains categorized questions that faculty should answer to help the course run smoothly and minimize misunderstandings.
Initial Introduction
- What are your expectations for initial meetings?
- How will you introduce yourself and learn about your TAs?
- What background information about the course will you share?
Roles and Responsibilities
- What tasks will TAs handle independently?
- What requires your approval or input?
- How will you divide responsibilities among multiple TAs?
- What are the boundaries of TA authority with students?
Communication Protocol
- What is your preferred communication method?
- What is your expected response time?
- When should TAs escalate issues to you?
- How often will you meet (weekly? As needed?)?
Course Objectives and Content
- What are the main learning goals for students?
- What key concepts are essential vs. supplementary?
- What common misconceptions should TAs watch for?
- How does this course fit in the broader curriculum?
Course Management
- How should TAs handle late work?
- What is the policy for missed classes or extensions?
- How should TAs respond to grade disputes?
- What accommodations might students have?
Grading Protocols
- Will you use rubrics? Who creates them?
- What is the calibration process for consistent grading?
- How should TAs handle borderline cases?
- What is the turnaround time expectation for grading?
Midterm and Final Exam Grading
- How will exam grading be divided?
- What is the process for handling potential cheating?
- How should TAs document grading decisions?
Student Observations and Feedback
- How should TAs report concerning student behavior?
- What early warning signs should TAs flag?
- How will mid-semester feedback be collected and used?
TA Professional Development
- Will TAs have opportunities to lead discussions or lectures?
- What teaching feedback will you provide?
- Are there workshops or training you recommend?
- How can this experience support their career goals?
Teaching Reflections
- How will you debrief after challenging classes?
- What opportunities exist for TAs to share ideas?
- How will you handle disagreements about teaching approaches?
Getting Started
Customize this checklist to your specific course and context. Meeting with TAs before the semester starts to discuss these topics sets everyone up for success.

title: HyFlex Teaching: Designing for Flexible Modalities
summary: HyFlex courses let students choose between in-person and online participation. Here's how to design equivalent experiences across modalities.
content_plain:
HyFlex courses let students choose between in-person and online participation. Here's how to design equivalent experiences across modalities. HyFlex is a course mode that gives students the flexibility to choose between on-site and online instruction within the same course. Students can move between modalities throughout the course as needed.
Core Principles of HyFlex
1. Learner Choice
Students decide their participation mode based on their circumstances. This might change week to week or even session to session.
2. Equivalence
Learning objectives are the same regardless of attendance mode. Students shouldn't be disadvantaged by their choice.
3. Reusability
Course materials created for one modality can be used by students in another. Recorded sessions serve both synchronous and asynchronous learners.
4. Accessibility
Design must accommodate all learners, regardless of how they're participating.
Designing for HyFlex
Synchronous Components
- In-person attendees: Traditional classroom participation
- Online synchronous attendees: Video conference access to the same session
- Consider: Camera placement, microphone quality, chat monitoring
Asynchronous Components
- Recorded sessions: Available for students who can't attend either live option
- Discussion forums: Allow participation regardless of schedule
- Self-paced activities: Work that can be completed anytime
Common Challenges and Solutions
Challenge: In-person and online students feel like separate classes
Solution: 
- Use breakout rooms that mix modalities
- Have online students contribute via chat that you read aloud
- Design activities where both groups collaborate
Challenge: Online students feel like second-class citizens
Solution:
- Address the camera directly, not just the room
- Assign a TA or student to monitor chat
- Ensure online students can hear in-person questions
Challenge: Recording adds burden
Solution:
- Use automatic recording in your video conferencing tool
- Don't edit—students prefer authentic to polished
- Consider recording only selected sessions
Technology Setup
Essential Equipment
- Camera positioned to capture both you and the board/screen
- Quality microphone that picks up room audio
- Second monitor to see online participants
Recommended Practices
- Test your setup before the semester starts
- Have a backup plan for technology failures
- Provide clear instructions for students on how to participate
Making the Choice Meaningful
HyFlex works best when:
- Students have genuine choice (not forced online due to capacity limits)
- Both options are truly equivalent
- The flexibility addresses real student needs
Consider: Is HyFlex the right model for your course, or would a different flexible approach work better?

title: Creating Effective Microlectures
summary: Short, focused video lectures (3-7 minutes) that target specific learning goals or common stumbling blocks—best practices for creation and integration.
content_plain:
Short, focused video lectures (3-7 minutes) that target specific learning goals or common stumbling blocks—best practices for creation and integration. Microlectures are short video lectures, typically 3-7 minutes long, focused on a single learning objective or concept. They're particularly useful for addressing specific points where students struggle.
When to Use Microlectures
Prerequisite Knowledge
Create microlectures covering foundational concepts students need before class. This levels the playing field and makes class time more productive.
Common Stumbling Blocks
Identify concepts where students consistently struggle. A microlecture available 24/7 lets students revisit explanations on their own schedule.
Worked Examples
Walk through problem solutions step-by-step. Students can pause, rewind, and rewatch as many times as needed.
Flipped Classroom Preparation
Move content delivery to microlectures so class time can focus on application, discussion, and practice.
Design Principles
Keep It Focused
One learning goal per microlecture. Avoid tangents, fun facts, or information not directly related to the objective.
Keep It Short
Research shows attention drops significantly after 6-9 minutes. If your topic needs more time, split it into multiple videos.
Make It Scannable
Provide a brief outline at the start so students know what's coming. Use clear verbal signposts: "First... Second... Finally..."
Plan for Rewatching
Students will pause and rewind. Design with this in mind—don't rush through key points.
Production Tips
Equipment
- Your laptop webcam and microphone are usually sufficient
- Screen recording tools (like Kaltura, Loom, or OBS) capture your screen and voice
- Write on a tablet if you need to show handwriting
Recording
- Script or outline your content first
- Do a test recording to check audio levels
- Record in a quiet space with good lighting
- It's okay to record in segments and piece them together
Quality Threshold
Don't obsess over production value. Students care more about clear explanation than professional polish. "Good enough" is genuinely good enough.
Integration Strategies
With Embedded Questions
Use tools like PlayPosit to add questions that pause the video. This increases engagement and checks understanding.
As Part of Assignments
Have students watch the microlecture, then complete a related task and submit it before class.
For Just-in-Time Support
Link to relevant microlectures in assignment instructions so students can access help exactly when they need it.
Assessment
Track viewing analytics to see:
- What percentage of students watch?
- Where do students pause or rewatch?
- Are there correlations between viewing and performance?
Use this data to improve both your microlectures and your understanding of student learning.
